title: Financial Statement Analysis Framework
description: Understand the objectives and tools of financial statement analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Financial statement analysis converts raw numbers into insights. CMAs use these techniques to assess company performance, evaluate creditworthiness, compare competitors, and identify trends. This is foundational for all strategic financial decisions.

sections.title: Objectives of Financial Analysis
sections.content:
**Who analyzes and why:**

**Investors:** Assess value, growth potential, risk
**Creditors:** Evaluate ability to repay, collateral
**Management:** Monitor performance, plan improvements
**Suppliers:** Assess creditworthiness
**Employees:** Evaluate job security, negotiate compensation
**Regulators:** Ensure compliance, protect stakeholders

**The same statements serve different purposes!**

sections.title: Horizontal Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Comparing financial data across time periods.

**Trend analysis:**
• Dollar change = Current Year - Base Year
• Percent change = (Current - Base) / Base × 100

**Example:**
Revenue 2025: $1,200,000
Revenue 2024: $1,000,000

Dollar change: $200,000
Percent change: ($200,000 / $1,000,000) × 100 = **20% growth**

**Use for:** Identifying trends, growth patterns, anomalies

sections.title: Vertical Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Expressing each line item as a percentage of a base amount within the same period.

**Income Statement:** Base = Revenue (100%)
• Gross profit as % of revenue
• Operating expenses as % of revenue
• Net income as % of revenue

**Balance Sheet:** Base = Total Assets (100%)
• Each asset as % of total assets
• Each liability/equity as % of total assets

**Use for:** Understanding structure, comparisons across company sizes

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: H vs. V Analysis
sections.content:
**"H = Horizon (time), V = View (structure)"**

**H**orizontal = Across time (Year 1 → Year 2 → Year 3)
**V**ertical = Within one period (line items as % of base)

**Common-size statements use vertical analysis - makes comparison easy regardless of company size!**

sections.title: Analysis Interpretation
sections.content:
**From the table above:**

Company A (smaller) vs. Company B (4× larger):
• A has higher gross margin (40% vs. 30%)
• A has lower operating efficiency (20% vs. 15%)
• A has higher net profit margin (12% vs. 9%)

**Insight:** Company A is more profitable per dollar of sales despite being smaller. Its premium pricing or cost structure outweighs the efficiency gains of Company B's scale.

**Common-size allows apple-to-apples comparison!**

sections.title: Limitations of Financial Analysis
sections.content:
**Key cautions:**

• **Historical data:** Past performance ≠ future results
• **Accounting methods:** Different policies distort comparisons
• **Window dressing:** Companies may manage period-end numbers
• **Industry differences:** Cross-industry comparison limited
• **Non-financial factors:** Don't capture culture, innovation, risk
• **Single point in time:** Balance sheet is a snapshot

**Analysis provides insights, not answers!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Horizontal analysis compares across time periods
Vertical analysis expresses items as % of base in same period
Common-size statements enable comparison regardless of size
Income statement base = Revenue; Balance sheet base = Total Assets
Different users analyze for different purposes
Analysis has limitations - accounting methods, historical bias
Combine multiple techniques for comprehensive understanding

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following stakeholders would PRIMARILY use financial statement analysis to assess a company's ability to meet its short-term obligations and the potential risk associated with extending credit?
options: Management aiming to improve operational efficiency.
Suppliers evaluating creditworthiness before extending trade credit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Suppliers are directly concerned with a company's ability to pay for goods or services rendered in the short term. Assessing creditworthiness minimizes the risk of non-payment. Investors focus on long-term growth, management on operations, and employees on compensation and job security.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which stakeholder has the most immediate financial risk related to the company's solvency.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) was $600,000 in 2024 and $750,000 in 2025. Using horizontal analysis, what is the percentage change in COGS from 2024 to 2025?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The formula for percentage change is ((Current Year - Base Year) / Base Year) * 100. In this case, (($750,000 - $600,000) / $600,000) * 100 = 25%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to divide the dollar change by the base year amount.

reveal.question: In vertical analysis, what base amount is typically used for the income statement, and what base amount is typically used for the balance sheet?
reveal.answer: Income Statement: Revenue (Net Sales). Balance Sheet: Total Assets.

rows.label: Focus
values: Relationship of items within a period

rows.label: Base
values: A key item within the same period (e.g., Revenue, Total Assets)

rows.label: Calculation
values: Dollar and percentage changes across periods
Percentage of a base amount within the same period

rows.label: Primary Use
values: Assess the relative size and contribution of individual items

title: Liquidity Ratios
description: Measure short-term solvency and ability to meet current obligations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Can the company pay its bills? Liquidity ratios answer this critical question. CMAs monitor these ratios to prevent cash crises, negotiate with creditors, and ensure operations continue smoothly. Running out of cash kills otherwise healthy companies!

sections.title: Current Ratio
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities

**Interpretation:**
• Measures ability to pay short-term obligations
• Higher = more liquidity cushion
• Generally, > 1.0 means more assets than liabilities

**Example:**
Current Assets: $500,000
Current Liabilities: $250,000
Current Ratio: $500,000 / $250,000 = **2.0**

**For every $1 of current debt, there's $2 of current assets.**

sections.title: Quick Ratio (Acid Test)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Quick Ratio = (Cash + Marketable Securities + Receivables) / Current Liabilities

**OR:** (Current Assets - Inventory - Prepaids) / Current Liabilities

**Why exclude inventory?**
• Inventory may be slow to convert to cash
• May need to sell at discount
• More conservative measure

**Example:**
Cash: $100,000, AR: $150,000, Inventory: $200,000, CL: $250,000
Quick Ratio: ($100,000 + $150,000) / $250,000 = **1.0**

sections.title: Cash Ratio
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Cash Ratio = (Cash + Marketable Securities) / Current Liabilities

**Most conservative liquidity measure:**
• Only includes most liquid assets
• Excludes receivables (collection risk)
• Worst-case scenario measure

**Example:**
Cash: $100,000, CL: $250,000
Cash Ratio: $100,000 / $250,000 = **0.4**

**Industry norms vary widely!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Liquidity Ratios
sections.content:
**"CQC" - From broad to narrow:**

**C**urrent = ALL current assets (broadest)
**Q**uick = Current minus inventory/prepaids (tighter)
**C**ash = Only cash and marketable securities (tightest)

**As you go down, you get more conservative!**

sections.title: Working Capital
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities

**This is a dollar amount, not a ratio!**

**What it means:**
• Positive WC: Assets exceed liabilities (cushion)
• Negative WC: Liabilities exceed assets (risk)
• The "buffer" for operations

**Example:**
Current Assets: $500,000
Current Liabilities: $250,000
Working Capital: **$250,000**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Industry Differences
sections.content:
**"Good" ratios vary by industry!**

**High inventory businesses (retail):**
• Lower quick ratio is normal
• Focus on inventory turnover

**Service businesses:**
• Little inventory
• Quick ratio ≈ Current ratio

**Subscription businesses:**
• Deferred revenue inflates current liabilities
• May have low current ratio but strong cash flows

**Always compare to industry peers!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities (broadest)
Quick Ratio excludes inventory (more conservative)
Cash Ratio includes only cash/securities (most conservative)
Working Capital is a dollar amount, not a ratio
Higher liquidity = more cushion but potentially inefficient
Industry norms vary significantly
Low liquidity can kill profitable companies

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has current assets of $800,000, including inventory of $200,000. Current liabilities are $500,000. What is the company's current ratio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The current ratio is calculated as Current Assets / Current Liabilities. In this case, $800,000 / $500,000 = 1.6.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula for the current ratio.

reveal.question: List the components included in the numerator of the Quick Ratio.
reveal.answer: Cash, Marketable Securities, and Accounts Receivable. Alternatively, Current Assets minus Inventory and Prepaid Expenses.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's cash ratio is 0.4. This indicates that:
options: The company has $0.40 of current assets for every $1 of current liabilities.
The company has $0.40 of cash and marketable securities for every $1 of current liabilities.
The company's current liabilities are 40% of its cash and marketable securities.
The company is highly liquid and has no risk of short-term financial distress.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The cash ratio measures the company's ability to pay its current liabilities with its most liquid assets (cash and marketable securities). A ratio of 0.4 means that for every $1 of current liabilities, the company has $0.40 in cash and marketable securities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider what assets are used in the calculation of the cash ratio.

title: Activity Ratios
description: Measure operational efficiency and asset utilization

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How efficiently does the company use its assets? Activity ratios answer this. CMAs use these to identify slow-moving inventory, collection problems, and underutilized assets. Improvement here directly boosts ROI and cash flow!

sections.title: Inventory Turnover
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Inventory Turnover = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory

**Days in Inventory:**
365 / Inventory Turnover

**Example:**
COGS: $600,000
Average Inventory: $100,000
Turnover: $600,000 / $100,000 = **6.0 times**
Days: 365 / 6 = **61 days**

**Interpretation:** Inventory sells and is replaced 6 times per year (every 61 days).

sections.title: Receivables Turnover
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Receivables Turnover = Net Credit Sales / Average Accounts Receivable

**Days Sales Outstanding (DSO):**
365 / Receivables Turnover

**Example:**
Credit Sales: $1,000,000
Average AR: $125,000
Turnover: $1,000,000 / $125,000 = **8.0 times**
DSO: 365 / 8 = **46 days**

**Interpretation:** Customers pay every 46 days on average.

sections.title: Payables Turnover
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Payables Turnover = Purchases (or COGS) / Average Accounts Payable

**Days Payable Outstanding (DPO):**
365 / Payables Turnover

**Example:**
Purchases: $500,000
Average AP: $50,000
Turnover: $500,000 / $50,000 = **10.0 times**
DPO: 365 / 10 = **37 days**

**Interpretation:** Company pays suppliers every 37 days on average.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Cash Conversion Cycle
sections.content:
**CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO**

**D**ays **I**nventory **O**utstanding (how long to sell)
+ **D**ays **S**ales **O**utstanding (how long to collect)
- **D**ays **P**ayable **O**utstanding (how long to pay suppliers)
= **C**ash **C**onversion **C**ycle

**Lower CCC = Better!** (Cash tied up for fewer days)

**Example:** 61 + 46 - 37 = 70 days CCC

sections.title: Total Asset Turnover Example
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Total Asset Turnover = Sales / Average Total Assets

**Example:**
Sales: $2,000,000
Average Total Assets: $1,000,000
Turnover: $2,000,000 / $1,000,000 = **2.0 times**

**Interpretation:** Each dollar of assets generates $2 of sales.

**This is the "Asset Turnover" in DuPont analysis!**
ROI = Profit Margin × Asset Turnover

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Average vs. Ending
sections.content:
**Use AVERAGE for balance sheet items in activity ratios!**

**Why?**
• Income statement covers a period
• Balance sheet is a point in time
• Average better matches flow to stock

**Average = (Beginning + Ending) / 2**

**If problem only gives ending balance:**
• Use that (state your assumption)
• Some exam questions give only ending

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Activity ratios measure how efficiently assets are used
Inventory Turnover = COGS / Avg Inventory
Receivables Turnover = Credit Sales / Avg AR
Payables Turnover = Purchases / Avg AP
CCC = DIO + DSO - DPO (lower is better)
Asset Turnover = Sales / Avg Assets (DuPont component)
Use AVERAGE balance sheet values

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a Cost of Goods Sold of $1,200,000. Its beginning inventory was $150,000 and its ending inventory was $250,000. What is the company's inventory turnover ratio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The average inventory is ($150,000 + $250,000) / 2 = $200,000. The inventory turnover ratio is $1,200,000 / $200,000 = 6.0 times.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate the *average* inventory first.

reveal.question: What is the formula for calculating Days Sales Outstanding (DSO)?
reveal.answer: DSO = 365 / Receivables Turnover, where Receivables Turnover = Net Credit Sales / Average Accounts Receivable.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's Cost of Goods Sold is $800,000. They purchased $750,000 worth of goods during the year. Their beginning Accounts Payable was $60,000 and their ending Accounts Payable was $90,000. What is the company's Days Payable Outstanding (DPO)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate average accounts payable: ($60,000 + $90,000) / 2 = $75,000.  Then, calculate payables turnover: $750,000 / $75,000 = 10 times. Finally, DPO = 365 / 10 = 36.5 days.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use Purchases, not COGS, in the Payables Turnover calculation. If purchases are not given, COGS can be used as a proxy.

title: Leverage Ratios
description: Analyze capital structure and long-term solvency

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How much debt is too much? Leverage ratios answer this critical capital structure question. CMAs use these to assess financial risk, evaluate financing options, and ensure debt covenants are met. Excessive leverage has destroyed many companies!

sections.title: Debt Ratio
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Debt Ratio = Total Liabilities / Total Assets

**Interpretation:**
• What portion of assets is financed by debt?
• Higher ratio = more financial leverage/risk
• 50% means half of assets funded by debt

**Example:**
Total Liabilities: $400,000
Total Assets: $1,000,000
Debt Ratio: $400,000 / $1,000,000 = **40%**

**60% is financed by equity**

sections.title: Debt-to-Equity Ratio
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Debt-to-Equity = Total Liabilities / Total Equity

**Alternative (long-term only):**
Long-term Debt / Total Equity

**Example:**
Total Liabilities: $400,000
Total Equity: $600,000
D/E Ratio: $400,000 / $600,000 = **0.67** (or 67%)

**Interpretation:** For every $1 of equity, there's $0.67 of debt.

**D/E > 1.0 means more debt than equity!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Debt vs. D/E
sections.content:
**Same numerator, different denominator!**

**Debt Ratio:** Liabilities / **Assets** (% of assets financed by debt)
**D/E Ratio:** Liabilities / **Equity** (debt relative to equity)

**Converting between them:**
If Debt Ratio = 40%, then D/E = 40/60 = 0.67
If D/E = 1.0, then Debt Ratio = 50%

**(Assets = Liabilities + Equity, so they're mathematically related)**

sections.title: Interest Coverage Ratios
rows: How many times can we pay interest?

sections.title: Times Interest Earned Example
sections.content:
**Formula:**
TIE = EBIT / Interest Expense

**Example:**
EBIT: $500,000
Interest Expense: $100,000
TIE: $500,000 / $100,000 = **5.0 times**

**Interpretation:** Earnings cover interest 5 times over.

**Rule of thumb:**
• TIE < 1.5: Danger zone
• TIE 2-3: Adequate
• TIE > 5: Strong coverage

**Debt covenants often require minimum TIE!**

sections.title: Equity Multiplier
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Equity Multiplier = Total Assets / Total Equity

**Example:**
Total Assets: $1,000,000
Total Equity: $600,000
Equity Multiplier: $1,000,000 / $600,000 = **1.67**

**Interpretation:**
• For every $1 of equity, assets are $1.67
• The difference is debt financing
• Higher multiplier = more leverage

**Used in DuPont extended formula:**
ROE = Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Operating vs. Finance Leases
sections.content:
**Under ASC 842, most leases are on balance sheet!**

**This affects leverage ratios:**
• Lease liabilities increase total liabilities
• Right-of-use assets increase total assets
• Debt ratios may increase

**Compare carefully when:**
• Analyzing pre- vs. post-ASC 842 data
• Comparing companies that adopted differently
• Evaluating debt covenants

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Debt Ratio = Total Liabilities / Total Assets
D/E Ratio = Total Liabilities / Total Equity (D/E > 1.0 = more debt than equity)
TIE = EBIT / Interest (how many times earnings cover interest)
Equity Multiplier = Assets / Equity (DuPont component)
Higher leverage = higher risk but potentially higher ROE
Debt covenants often impose leverage limits
Lease accounting (ASC 842) affects leverage ratios

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X has total liabilities of $750,000 and total assets of $1,500,000. Which of the following statements is MOST accurate regarding Company X's financial leverage?
options: Company X has a debt ratio of 50%, indicating that half of its assets are financed by debt.
Company X has a debt ratio of 200%, indicating that its liabilities are twice its assets.
Company X has a debt ratio of 2%, indicating very low financial risk.
Company X has a debt ratio of 0.5%, indicating very low financial risk.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The debt ratio is calculated as Total Liabilities / Total Assets. In this case, $750,000 / $1,500,000 = 0.50 or 50%. A debt ratio of 50% means that half of the company's assets are financed by debt.  The higher the debt ratio, the more financial leverage and risk the company has.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Debt Ratio = Total Liabilities / Total Assets. What does a higher ratio signify?

reveal.question: What does a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.80 mean?
reveal.answer: A debt-to-equity ratio of 0.80 means that for every $1 of equity, the company has $0.80 of debt. It indicates the extent to which a company is using debt to finance its assets relative to the value of shareholders' equity. A higher ratio generally suggests a more aggressive financing strategy and potentially higher risk.

rows.label: Interpretation
values: Proportion of assets financed by debt.
Amount of debt for each dollar of equity.

rows.label: Risk Assessment
values: Indicates the degree to which a company's assets are funded by debt; higher = higher risk
Indicates the extent to which shareholder investment is leveraged by debt; higher = higher risk

title: Profitability Ratios
description: Measure ability to generate profits from operations and investments

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Are we making money? Profitability ratios are the ultimate test of business success. CMAs track these to evaluate strategy effectiveness, compare to competitors, and identify improvement areas. Investors and lenders focus heavily on these metrics.

sections.title: Margin Analysis Example
sections.content:
**Income Statement:**
Revenue: $1,000,000
COGS: $600,000 → Gross Profit: $400,000
Operating Expenses: $250,000 → Operating Income: $150,000
Interest: $20,000, Taxes: $32,500 → Net Income: $97,500

**Margins:**
• Gross Margin: $400,000 / $1,000,000 = **40%**
• Operating Margin: $150,000 / $1,000,000 = **15%**
• Net Profit Margin: $97,500 / $1,000,000 = **9.75%**

**Trend these over time and benchmark against competitors!**

sections.title: Return on Assets (ROA)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
ROA = Net Income / Average Total Assets

**Alternative (operating):**
ROA = EBIT(1-t) / Average Total Assets

**Example:**
Net Income: $97,500
Average Total Assets: $750,000
ROA: $97,500 / $750,000 = **13%**

**Interpretation:** Each dollar of assets generates $0.13 of profit.

**DuPont breakdown:**
ROA = Net Profit Margin × Asset Turnover
13% = 9.75% × 1.33

sections.title: Return on Equity (ROE)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
ROE = Net Income / Average Shareholders' Equity

**Example:**
Net Income: $97,500
Average Equity: $450,000
ROE: $97,500 / $450,000 = **21.7%**

**Interpretation:** Each dollar of equity generates $0.217 of profit.

**Why ROE > ROA?**
Financial leverage! Borrowing allows more assets per dollar of equity.

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: DuPont Analysis
sections.content:
**3-Part DuPont for ROE:**

ROE = Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier

**"How profitable?" × "How efficient?" × "How leveraged?"**

**Example:**
ROE = 9.75% × 1.33 × 1.67 = 21.7% ✓

This shows THREE ways to improve ROE:
1. Better margins (operations)
2. Better turnover (efficiency)
3. More leverage (risk!)

sections.title: Return on Invested Capital (ROIC)
sections.content:
**Formula:**
ROIC = NOPAT / Invested Capital

**Where:**
• NOPAT = EBIT × (1 - Tax Rate)
• Invested Capital = Equity + Debt - Cash

**Why ROIC?**
• Measures operating performance
• Excludes financing effects (interest)
• Comparable across capital structures

**Compare ROIC to WACC:**
• ROIC > WACC → Creating value
• ROIC < WACC → Destroying value

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Which Numerator?
sections.content:
**Match numerator to denominator!**

**Net Income** → Use with Equity (ROE)
(Net income is after interest = belongs to shareholders)

**EBIT or NOPAT** → Use with Total Assets or Invested Capital
(Pre-interest = belongs to all capital providers)

**Mixing these creates apples-to-oranges comparisons!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Gross Margin measures production efficiency
Operating Margin measures operational efficiency
Net Profit Margin is bottom-line profitability
ROA = Net Income / Avg Assets (or NOPAT / Assets)
ROE = Net Income / Avg Equity
DuPont: ROE = Margin × Turnover × Leverage
ROIC compared to WACC indicates value creation

knowledgeCheck.question: Using the income statement provided in the lesson, what is the Operating Margin?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Operating Margin is calculated as Operating Income / Revenue.  In this case, $150,000 / $1,000,000 = 15%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the formula: Operating Income divided by Revenue.

reveal.question: State the two formulas for Return on Assets (ROA).
reveal.answer:
ROA = Net Income / Average Total Assets  

OR  

ROA = EBIT(1-t) / Average Total Assets

knowledgeCheck.question: Company X has a Net Income of $500,000 and Average Total Assets of $2,000,000. Which of the following statements is the MOST accurate interpretation of the company's ROA?
options: For every dollar of revenue, the company generates $0.25 of profit.
For every dollar of assets, the company generates $0.25 of profit.
The company's assets are growing at a rate of 25% per year.
The company is using leverage effectively to generate a 25% return for shareholders.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: ROA measures the return generated from assets.  A ROA of 25% ($500,000/$2,000,000) means that for every dollar of assets, the company generates $0.25 of profit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on what ROA measures: the return generated from the company's *assets*.

rows.label: Impact of Leverage
values: Directly impacted by leverage (financial leverage increases ROE)

title: Market Value Ratios
description: Analyze stock performance and market valuation metrics

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: How does the market value the company? Market ratios connect financial performance to stock price. CMAs use these to evaluate compensation plans (stock options), communicate with investors, and assess relative valuation for M&A analysis.

sections.title: Earnings Per Share (EPS)
sections.content:
**Basic EPS:**
(Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares

**Example:**
Net Income: $1,000,000
Preferred Dividends: $50,000
Weighted Avg Shares: 200,000
Basic EPS: ($1,000,000 - $50,000) / 200,000 = **$4.75**

**Diluted EPS:**
Accounts for potential shares (options, convertibles)
Always ≤ Basic EPS (dilution reduces EPS)

sections.title: Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio
sections.content:
**Formula:**
P/E = Stock Price / EPS

**Example:**
Stock Price: $57.00
EPS: $4.75
P/E: $57.00 / $4.75 = **12.0x**

**Interpretation:**
• Investors pay $12 for each $1 of earnings
• Higher P/E = higher growth expectations
• Compare to industry average and historical

**Trailing P/E:** Using last 12 months EPS
**Forward P/E:** Using next 12 months estimated EPS

sections.title: Book Value Per Share
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Book Value Per Share = (Total Equity - Preferred Equity) / Common Shares Outstanding

**Example:**
Common Equity: $2,000,000
Shares Outstanding: 200,000
BVPS: $2,000,000 / 200,000 = **$10.00**

**Price-to-Book (P/B):**
Stock Price / BVPS = $57 / $10 = **5.7x**

**P/B > 1:** Market values company above book value (intangibles, growth)
**P/B < 1:** May indicate undervaluation or problems

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: P/E Interpretation
sections.content:
**"GRRR" - Higher P/E means:**

**G**rowth expected to be higher
**R**isk perceived to be lower
**R**equired return is lower
**R**eturn on equity is higher

**But watch out:** Could also mean stock is overvalued!

**Always ask: "Is the P/E justified by fundamentals?"**

sections.title: Dividend Ratios
rows: What % of earnings paid as dividends

sections.title: Dividend Metrics Example
sections.content:
**Given:**
• Net Income: $950,000
• Dividends Paid: $285,000
• Stock Price: $57.00
• Shares Outstanding: 200,000

**Calculations:**
DPS: $285,000 / 200,000 = **$1.425**

Dividend Yield: $1.425 / $57.00 = **2.5%**

Payout Ratio: $285,000 / $950,000 = **30%**

Retention Ratio: 1 - 30% = **70%**

**Company retains 70% for reinvestment!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Weighted Average Shares
sections.content:
**For EPS, use WEIGHTED AVERAGE shares!**

**Example:**
Jan 1: 100,000 shares outstanding
July 1: Issued 20,000 shares
Dec 31: Year end

Weighted Average:
100,000 × (12/12) + 20,000 × (6/12)
= 100,000 + 10,000 = **110,000 shares**

**Stock splits/dividends are applied retroactively!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Basic EPS = (Net Income - Preferred Div) / Weighted Avg Shares
P/E = Price / EPS (higher = higher growth expectations)
Book Value Per Share = Common Equity / Shares
P/B > 1 indicates market premium over book value
Dividend Yield = DPS / Price (income return)
Payout Ratio + Retention Ratio = 100%
Use weighted average shares for EPS calculations

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Corp. had a net income of $2,500,000, preferred dividends of $250,000, and a weighted average of 500,000 common shares outstanding. Additionally, Apex has convertible bonds that, if converted, would increase the weighted average shares outstanding by 50,000. What is Apex Corp.'s basic Earnings Per Share (EPS)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Basic EPS is calculated as (Net Income - Preferred Dividends) / Weighted Average Common Shares. In this case, ($2,500,000 - $250,000) / 500,000 = $4.50. The convertible bonds are used in calculating diluted EPS, not basic EPS.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to only use information relevant to *basic* EPS.

reveal.question: What does a high Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio generally indicate to investors?
reveal.answer: A high P/E ratio suggests that investors are willing to pay a premium for each dollar of earnings, often reflecting higher growth expectations for the company compared to companies with lower P/E ratios. It can also indicate that the stock is overvalued.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has total equity of $5,000,000, preferred equity of $1,000,000, and 400,000 common shares outstanding. If the company's stock is trading at $60 per share, what is the Price-to-Book (P/B) ratio?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: First, calculate Book Value Per Share: ($5,000,000 - $1,000,000) / 400,000 = $10. Then, calculate the P/B ratio: $60 / $10 = 6.0x.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember the order of operations! Calculate Book Value Per Share first.

title: Cash Flow Analysis
description: Analyze cash flows and assess cash generation capability

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Cash is king. Profitable companies can fail if they run out of cash. Cash flow analysis reveals whether earnings are backed by real cash and whether the company can fund operations, investments, and debt payments. CMAs must go beyond accrual earnings to understand cash reality.

sections.title: Cash Flow Statement Structure
sections.content:
**Three Sections:**

**Operating Activities (CFO):**
Cash from core business operations

**Investing Activities (CFI):**
Cash for/from long-term assets
(CapEx, acquisitions, asset sales)

**Financing Activities (CFF):**
Cash from/to capital providers
(Debt, equity, dividends)

**Net Change = CFO + CFI + CFF**

**Healthy pattern:** +CFO, -CFI, +/- CFF

sections.title: Free Cash Flow
sections.content:
**Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF):**
CFO - Capital Expenditures

**Or from EBIT:**
EBIT(1-t) + D&A - CapEx - Δ Working Capital

**Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE):**
FCFF - Interest(1-t) - Principal Payments + New Borrowing

**What FCF means:**
Cash available after maintaining/growing operations
Can be used for dividends, debt paydown, buybacks, acquisitions

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: FCF Uses
sections.content:
**"DRAB"** - What to do with Free Cash Flow:

**D**ividends to shareholders
**R**epay debt
**A**cquisitions/investments
**B**uybacks (share repurchases)

**FCF is what's left after keeping the business running!**

sections.title: Cash Flow Ratios
rows: Ability to fund investment from operations

sections.title: Quality of Earnings
sections.content:
**Does cash flow support reported earnings?**

**CFO / Net Income Ratio:**
• Ratio > 1: Cash exceeds accrual earnings (positive)
• Ratio ≈ 1: Cash matches earnings (normal)
• Ratio < 1: Earnings not backed by cash (caution)

**Warning signs:**
• Growing gap between CFO and Net Income
• Receivables growing faster than sales
• Inventory building faster than COGS
• Aggressive revenue recognition

**Accrual earnings can be manipulated; cash is harder to fake!**

sections.title: Cash Burn Rate
sections.content:
**For loss-making companies:**

**Monthly Burn Rate:**
Negative CFO / 12 months

**Cash Runway:**
Cash & Equivalents / Monthly Burn Rate

**Example:**
Cash: $10,000,000
Annual CFO: -$6,000,000
Monthly Burn: $500,000
Runway: $10,000,000 / $500,000 = **20 months**

**Company has 20 months before cash runs out (without additional funding)**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Indirect Method Adjustments
sections.content:
**CFO indirect method starts with Net Income, then adjusts:**

**Add back:** Non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization)
**Subtract:** Non-cash gains (gain on sale)
**Add/Subtract:** Working capital changes

**Working capital changes:**
• Increase in CA (except cash) → SUBTRACT
• Decrease in CA → ADD
• Increase in CL → ADD
• Decrease in CL → SUBTRACT

**Remember: Changes in assets move OPPOSITE to cash!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CFO = Cash from operations (core business)
FCF = CFO - CapEx (cash after maintaining assets)
Healthy pattern: Positive CFO, negative CFI
CFO/Net Income > 1 indicates high-quality earnings
Warning: Earnings growing faster than cash
Burn rate and runway critical for unprofitable companies
Working capital increases consume cash

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following would be classified as a cash flow from operating activities (CFO)?
options: Purchase of a new manufacturing plant.
Sale of marketable securities held as a short-term investment.
Repurchase of the company's own stock.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Payment of salaries to employees directly relates to the core business operations of the company. The purchase of a plant is an investing activity, sale of securities is investing (if short-term), and stock repurchase is a financing activity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what activities are directly related to the company's day-to-day business.

reveal.question: What is the formula for Free Cash Flow to Firm (FCFF) starting with CFO?

knowledgeCheck.question: A company reports the following: FCFF = $500,000, Interest Expense = $50,000, Tax Rate = 30%, Principal Payments = $30,000, New Borrowing = $40,000. What is the Free Cash Flow to Equity (FCFE)?
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to adjust interest expense for the tax shield.

title: Comprehensive Ratio Analysis
description: Integrate ratios for complete company analysis using DuPont and other frameworks

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Individual ratios tell part of the story. CMAs must integrate ratios into a cohesive analysis that explains WHY performance is what it is and WHAT can be done about it. This is where analytical thinking adds real value!

sections.title: Extended DuPont Analysis
sections.content:
**5-Factor DuPont Model:**

ROE = (Net Income/EBT) × (EBT/EBIT) × (EBIT/Sales) × (Sales/Assets) × (Assets/Equity)

**Components:**
1. Tax Burden (NI/EBT)
2. Interest Burden (EBT/EBIT)
3. Operating Margin (EBIT/Sales)
4. Asset Turnover (Sales/Assets)
5. Equity Multiplier (Assets/Equity)

**This decomposes ROE into tax, financing, operations, efficiency, and leverage!**

sections.title: Sustainable Growth Rate
sections.content:
**Formula:**
SGR = ROE × Retention Ratio

**OR expanded:**
SGR = (Profit Margin × Asset Turnover × Equity Multiplier) × (1 - Payout Ratio)

**What it measures:**
Maximum growth rate maintainable without:
• Issuing new equity
• Changing capital structure
• Changing financial policies

**Example:**
ROE: 20%, Retention: 60%
SGR: 20% × 60% = **12%**

*Company can grow 12% while maintaining current leverage*

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SGR
sections.content:
**"SGR = How fast can we grow internally?"**

SGR = ROE × Retention Rate

**To grow FASTER than SGR:**
• Improve profitability (increase ROE)
• Retain more earnings (decrease dividends)
• Increase leverage (risky!)
• Issue new equity (dilution!)

**Actual Growth > SGR → Something has to give!**

sections.title: Integrated Analysis Framework
sections.content:
**Step 1: Start with ROE**
How much return are shareholders earning?

**Step 2: DuPont decomposition**
Is it from margins, efficiency, or leverage?

**Step 3: Drill into components**
• Low margin → Analyze gross vs. operating margin
• Low turnover → Check inventory, receivables, fixed assets
• High leverage → Review debt ratios, coverage

**Step 4: Cash flow reality check**
Does CFO support earnings?

**Step 5: Forward-looking assessment**
Sustainable? Improving or deteriorating?

sections.title: Common Patterns
rows: High margins, high reinvestment, low/no dividends
Focus on market share, may burn cash

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Extended DuPont breaks ROE into 5 factors
SGR = ROE × Retention Rate (max sustainable growth)
Growing faster than SGR requires policy changes
Integrate ratios - don't analyze in isolation
Cash flow is the reality check for accrual ratios
Look for patterns that indicate company type/stage
Trend analysis matters as much as point-in-time

knowledgeCheck.question: Using the 5-Factor DuPont model, which of the following components reflects the impact of debt financing on a company's Return on Equity (ROE)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Interest Burden (EBT/EBIT) reflects how effectively a company uses debt to increase its earnings relative to its earnings before interest and taxes. A lower interest burden suggests a higher proportion of earnings are being used to cover interest expenses, impacting ROE.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which component directly involves interest expense.

reveal.question: What are the three key restrictions or assumptions underlying the Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR)?
reveal.answer:
The SGR assumes that the company does NOT:
1. Issue new equity.
2. Change its capital structure (debt-to-equity ratio).
3. Alter its financial policies (dividend payout ratio).

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has a Return on Equity (ROE) of 15% and a dividend payout ratio of 40%. What is its Sustainable Growth Rate (SGR)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The SGR is calculated as ROE × Retention Ratio. The retention ratio is 1 - Payout Ratio = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60. Therefore, SGR = 15% × 0.60 = 9%.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember that the retention ratio is the portion of earnings not paid out as dividends.

title: Earnings Quality and Manipulation
description: Identify earnings manipulation and assess quality of reported results

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Reported earnings can be aggressive, conservative, or fraudulent. CMAs must assess earnings quality to avoid being misled and to ensure their own company's reporting is appropriate. Detecting manipulation early can save careers and companies!

sections.title: Dimensions of Earnings Quality
sections.content:
**High-quality earnings are:**

**Sustainable:** Will continue into future
**Cash-backed:** CFO supports accrual earnings
**Conservative:** Not relying on aggressive estimates
**Transparent:** Clear disclosure of methods
**GAAP-compliant:** Not pushing boundaries

**Low-quality earnings:**
• One-time items
• Aggressive accounting
• Growing accruals
• Complex transactions

sections.title: Revenue Recognition Red Flags
rows: Revenue growing faster than cash collections
Pulling sales forward to meet targets

sections.title: Accrual Analysis
sections.content:
**Total Accruals:**
(Change in Non-Cash Current Assets - Change in Current Liabilities - Depreciation) / Average Total Assets

**Interpretation:**
• High positive accruals → Earnings > Cash (caution)
• Negative accruals → Cash > Earnings (conservative)

**Accruals ratio trend:**
Increasing accruals over time is a warning sign!

**Beneish M-Score and other models use accruals as key input.**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Manipulation Red Flags
sections.content:
**"RECIPE"** for spotting manipulation:

**R**ecurring "one-time" items
**E**stimates that seem too favorable
**C**ash flow diverging from earnings
**I**nventory growing faster than sales
**P**olicy changes that boost income
**E**xecutive departures (especially CFO/Controller)

**Multiple flags together = higher concern!**

sections.title: Expense Manipulation
sections.content:
**Common techniques:**

**Capitalizing expenses:**
• R&D, software development, marketing
• Delays expense recognition

**Extending useful lives:**
• Reduces depreciation/amortization
• Assets may be overvalued

**Understating reserves:**
• Bad debts, warranties, litigation
• Inflates current earnings

**Cookie jar reserves:**
• Over-reserve in good years
• Release in bad years

**Compare policies to peers and historical practice!**

sections.title: Cash Flow Reality Check
sections.content:
**CFO to NI Comparison:**

Consistently CFO >> NI: Conservative accounting
Consistently CFO << NI: Aggressive accounting

**Growing gap over time:**
Earnings quality is deteriorating

**Specific checks:**
• Receivables growing faster than revenue
• Inventory growing faster than COGS
• Payables declining while activity stable
• Deferred revenue declining

**Cash doesn't lie - accruals can!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Aggressive ≠ Fraud
sections.content:
**Three levels of accounting:**

**Conservative:** Cautious estimates, earlier expense recognition

**Aggressive:** Optimistic estimates, push GAAP limits
(Still within GAAP - just at the edge)

**Fraudulent:** Violates GAAP, intent to deceive

**Aggressive accounting is not illegal, but:**
• May indicate future problems
• Can become fraud if taken further
• Often reverses in future periods

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Quality earnings are sustainable, cash-backed, conservative
Compare CFO to Net Income - divergence is a red flag
Watch for receivables/inventory growing faster than sales
Rising accruals over time indicates declining quality
"One-time" charges that recur are not one-time
Aggressive accounting isn't fraud but warrants caution
Multiple red flags together increase concern

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following characteristics is LEAST indicative of high-quality earnings?
options: Sustainability of earnings into future periods.
A strong, positive correlation between net income and operating cash flows.
Aggressive application of accounting estimates to maximize reported earnings.
Transparent and easily understandable disclosure of accounting methods and assumptions.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: High-quality earnings are characterized by conservatism, not aggressive accounting practices. Aggressive estimates, designed to artificially inflate earnings, are a red flag for low earnings quality and potential manipulation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what makes earnings reliable and trustworthy.

reveal.question: What does a significantly increasing accruals ratio trend typically indicate about a company's earnings quality?
reveal.answer: A significantly increasing accruals ratio trend suggests that a company's earnings are becoming increasingly reliant on accruals and less supported by cash flows from operations. This can be a red flag for potentially unsustainable earnings and earnings manipulation, as it indicates that the company is recognizing more revenue and/or deferring expenses than is justified by its cash flows.

rows.label: Sustainability
values: Sustainable - earnings expected to continue into the future
Unsustainable - earnings likely to be a one-time event or based on unsustainable practices

rows.label: Cash Flow
values: Cash-backed - supported by strong cash flows from operations
Not Cash-backed - earnings significantly exceed operating cash flows

rows.label: Conservatism
values: Aggressive - utilizes aggressive accounting estimates to inflate earnings

rows.label: Transparency
values: Opaque - difficult to understand disclosures or lack of transparency

title: Analytical Procedures in Practice
description: Apply financial analysis techniques to real-world situations

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Analysis without context is meaningless. CMAs must know how to benchmark against peers, analyze trends, and understand the limitations of ratio analysis. This lesson connects theory to practice - how to actually USE these tools.

sections.title: Benchmarking Approaches
sections.content:
**Internal benchmarking:**
• Prior periods (horizontal/trend analysis)
• Budget vs. actual
• Division vs. division

**External benchmarking:**
• Industry averages
• Direct competitors
• Best-in-class performers

**Sources of benchmarks:**
• Industry associations
• Financial databases (S&P, Moody's)
• Analysts' reports
• Company filings (peer comparison)

sections.title: Cross-Sectional Analysis
sections.content:
**Comparing companies at same point in time:**

**Select comparable companies:**
• Same industry
• Similar size
• Similar business model
• Similar geography

**Adjust for differences:**
• Accounting policies
• Fiscal year-ends
• Capital structure
• Growth stage

**Use common-size statements to neutralize size differences!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Analysis Framework
sections.content:
**"SCRIBE"** - Complete analysis approach:

**S**tructure (vertical analysis)
**C**hange (horizontal analysis)
**R**atios (comprehensive set)
**I**ndustry comparison (benchmark)
**B**usiness context (qualitative)
**E**xpectations (forward-looking)

**Don't skip any step!**

sections.title: Limitations of Ratio Analysis
sections.content:
**Key limitations:**

**Accounting differences:**
• LIFO vs. FIFO inventory
• Depreciation methods
• Operating vs. finance leases

**Comparability issues:**
• Conglomerates hard to classify
• Different fiscal years
• International standards vary

**Backward-looking:**
• Past may not predict future
• Misses qualitative factors

**Point-in-time issues:**
• Balance sheet is a snapshot
• Window dressing possible

sections.title: Practical Analysis Tips
sections.content:
**Don't:**
• Rely on single ratios
• Ignore industry context
• Forget cash flow analysis
• Accept numbers without verification
• Ignore qualitative factors

**Do:**
• Use multiple ratios together
• Analyze trends over time
• Compare to relevant peers
• Read footnotes and MD&A
• Consider business strategy
• Question unusual items
• Follow up on red flags

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Benchmark internally (trend) and externally (peers)
Use 3-5 years for trend analysis
Select truly comparable companies for external benchmarks
Adjust for accounting policy differences
Limitations: backward-looking, accounting differences, window dressing
Combine quantitative ratios with qualitative understanding
SCRIBE: Structure, Change, Ratios, Industry, Business, Expectations

knowledgeCheck.question: Apex Corp. is evaluating its current performance against its performance in the previous three years to identify trends in cost of goods sold. This is an example of:
options: Internal benchmarking using budget vs. actual analysis.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is (D). Comparing performance to prior periods is specifically defined as internal benchmarking using horizontal/trend analysis. Option A is incorrect because Apex Corp is comparing to its own prior performance, not an external entity. Option B is incorrect as Apex Corp is not evaluating the budget vs. actual. Option C is incorrect because Apex Corp is comparing to its own prior performance, not an external entity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the difference between internal and external benchmarking. Which one involves looking at your own past performance?

reveal.question: Name four factors that might need to be adjusted for when performing cross-sectional analysis.
reveal.answer:
The following are four factors that may need to be adjusted for:
1. Accounting policies
2. Fiscal year-ends
3. Capital structure
4. Growth stage

rows.label: Focus
values: Comparing performance within the organization (past or present).
Comparing performance against entities outside the organization (industry, competitors, best-in-class).

rows.label: Data Source
values: Internal accounting records, budgets, and operational data.
Industry reports, competitor filings, financial databases, and consulting reports.

rows.label: Primary Goal
values: Identify areas for improvement within the organization and track progress over time.
Identify best practices and performance gaps compared to external entities, informing strategic decisions.

title: Industry and Competitive Analysis
description: Apply financial statement analysis techniques to assess competitive position and industry trends

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Numbers in isolation are meaningless! CMAs must compare financial performance to industry peers and competitors to assess relative strength. Industry analysis reveals whether performance is company-specific or driven by broader market forces.

sections.title: Context Is Everything
sections.content:
**The Same Ratio Tells Different Stories:**

Net Profit Margin = 5%
• Grocery retail: Excellent (industry avg 2-3%)
• Software company: Poor (industry avg 20%+)
• Pharmaceutical: Below average (industry avg 15%)

**Always Compare To:**
• Industry averages
• Direct competitors
• Company's own history (trends)
• Management targets/guidance

sections.title: Sources of Industry Data
rows: Industry norms and key business ratios

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Analysis Framework
sections.content:
**"ITCH" - Four dimensions of comparison:**

**I**ndustry - How does the industry perform overall?
**T**rend - How has performance changed over time?
**C**ompetitors - How do we compare to direct rivals?
**H**istory - How do we compare to our own past?

Always analyze all four dimensions!

sections.title: Cross-Sectional Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:** Comparing ratios across companies at the same point in time

**Steps:**
1. Identify peer group (same industry, similar size)
2. Calculate key ratios for all peers
3. Rank performance and identify outliers
4. Investigate drivers of differences

**Peer Selection Criteria:**
• Same industry (NAICS/SIC code)
• Similar size (revenue, assets, market cap)
• Similar business model
• Same geographic markets

**Caution:** Different accounting policies can distort comparisons

sections.title: Time-Series (Trend) Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:** Tracking ratios over multiple periods to identify patterns

**What Trends Reveal:**
• Improving or deteriorating performance
• Cyclical patterns
• Impact of strategic initiatives
• Effects of external events

**Trend Calculation:**
Percentage Change = (Current - Prior) / Prior × 100

**Example:**
ROE: 12% → 14% → 15% → 18%
Positive trend indicates improving profitability

**Caution:** Past trends don't guarantee future performance

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Comparability Issues
sections.content:
**Factors that distort peer comparisons:**

**Accounting Differences:**
• LIFO vs. FIFO inventory
• Operating vs. finance lease treatment
• Revenue recognition timing
• Depreciation methods

**Business Differences:**
• Product mix variations
• Vertical integration levels
• Geographic diversification
• Fiscal year-end timing

**Solution:** Adjust for material differences before comparing

sections.title: Industry Life Cycle Analysis
rows: Negative profits, high investment, negative FCF
Peak profits, strong FCF, higher dividends
Declining revenue, cost cutting, harvesting assets

sections.title: Common-Size Analysis for Comparison
sections.content:
**Common-Size Income Statement:**
Every line item as % of Revenue

**Example Comparison:**
                    CompanyA    CompanyB    Industry
Gross Margin         45%         38%         40%
Operating Margin     20%         12%         15%
Net Margin           12%         6%          9%

**Analysis:** Company A outperforms on all margins
Company B lags industry — investigate cost structure

**Common-Size Balance Sheet:**
Every line item as % of Total Assets

Reveals asset intensity, leverage, liquidity differences

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Financial ratios require context - compare to industry, competitors, and trends
Same ratio value can be good or bad depending on industry
Cross-sectional analysis compares peers at same point in time
Time-series analysis tracks trends over multiple periods
Watch for comparability issues from accounting and business differences
Industry life cycle affects expected financial characteristics
Common-size statements facilitate comparison across different-sized companies

knowledgeCheck.question: A retail company has an inventory turnover of 8 times, while a software company has inventory turnover of 2 times. Which statement is MOST accurate?
options: The retail company is more efficient because it has higher turnover
The software company should improve its inventory management
Direct comparison is inappropriate due to different business models
Both companies are performing at industry-average levels
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Retail companies typically have high inventory turnover due to the nature of their business (selling physical goods). Software companies may have minimal inventory (if any). Comparing inventory turnover across different industries is not meaningful.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the business model differences between retail and software.

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's ROE has declined from 18% to 15% to 12% over three years, while the industry average ROE has remained stable at 14%. What is the BEST interpretation?
options: The company is still outperforming the industry
The deteriorating trend is concerning despite currently matching industry average
The decline is not significant since ROE is still positive
Industry factors are causing the decline
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While the current 12% ROE is close to the 14% industry average, the clear downward TREND is concerning. The company went from outperforming to underperforming. Management should investigate the cause before the situation worsens.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on the direction and speed of change, not just the current level.

reveal.question: What adjustments might an analyst make before comparing two companies in the same industry?
reveal.answer: Common adjustments include: (1) Converting LIFO to FIFO for inventory, (2) Capitalizing operating leases for leverage comparisons, (3) Adjusting for one-time or non-recurring items, (4) Standardizing pension assumptions, (5) Adjusting for different fiscal year-ends, and (6) Removing the effects of stock-based compensation.

title: SWOT Analysis for Strategic Planning
description: Master the SWOT framework for assessing strategic position and formulating strategy

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Strategic analysis isn't just for strategists! CMAs provide the data and financial perspective that makes SWOT analysis meaningful. Understanding strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats helps CMAs align financial planning with strategic realities.

sections.title: Internal Factors (S & W)
sections.content:
**Strengths - Internal Positives:**
• Strong brand recognition
• Low-cost production
• Skilled workforce
• Strong balance sheet
• Proprietary technology
• Customer loyalty

**Weaknesses - Internal Negatives:**
• High employee turnover
• Outdated technology
• Limited distribution
• Weak cash position
• Lack of scale
• Poor product quality

**Key:** These are things you can CONTROL

sections.title: External Factors (O & T)
sections.content:
**Opportunities - External Positives:**
• Growing market demand
• Competitor weakness
• Favorable regulations
• New technology
• Market expansion
• Partnership possibilities

**Threats - External Negatives:**
• New competitors
• Economic downturn
• Regulatory changes
• Changing customer preferences
• Supply chain disruption
• Technology disruption

**Key:** These are things you CANNOT control

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SWOT Categories
sections.content:
**S/W = Self (Internal)**
What are WE good or bad at?
Can be changed with effort

**O/T = Outside (External)**
What's happening around us?
Must respond and adapt

**Simple test:**
"Can we directly change this?"
Yes → Internal (S or W)
No → External (O or T)

sections.title: Using SWOT for Strategy
rows: AGGRESSIVE - Use strengths to capture opportunities
Use brand strength to enter new market
TURNAROUND - Fix weaknesses to capture opportunities
Upgrade technology to meet new demand
DIVERSIFICATION - Use strengths to counter threats
DEFENSIVE - Minimize weaknesses and avoid threats

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SWOT Pitfalls
sections.content:
**Common SWOT Mistakes:**

**1. Too Vague**
✘ "Good people"
✔ "Industry-low employee turnover of 5%"

**2. Wrong Category**
✘ "Strong economy" as Strength
✔ "Strong economy" is Opportunity (external)

**3. Opinion vs Fact**
Base on data and evidence, not assumptions

**4. Ignoring Connections**
Use SWOT combinations to generate strategies

**5. Static Analysis**
SWOT should be updated regularly

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SWOT divides factors into Internal (S/W) and External (O/T)
Helpful factors are Strengths and Opportunities
Harmful factors are Weaknesses and Threats
Internal factors can be controlled; external factors require adaptation
Combine SWOT quadrants to generate strategies
Be specific and evidence-based, not vague
Update SWOT regularly as conditions change

knowledgeCheck.question: A company identifies that a major competitor is facing financial difficulties and may exit the market. How should this be classified in a SWOT analysis?
options: Weakness - Company may lose benchmark for comparison
Opportunity - Potential to gain competitor's market share
Threat - Market instability from competitor exit
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A competitor's potential exit is an external factor (can't control it) that could benefit the company (potential to gain market share), making it an Opportunity. While competitor exit could also be a Threat in some contexts, gaining market share is the positive opportunity.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Is this internal or external? Is it helpful or harmful to the company?

title: Value Chain Analysis
description: Identify competitive advantage through systematic analysis of business activities

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Michael Porter's Value Chain framework helps CMAs understand where and how the company creates value. By analyzing each activity's costs and contributions, CMAs can identify opportunities for cost reduction, differentiation, and competitive advantage.

sections.title: Value Chain Concept
sections.content:
**Definition:** A company is a collection of activities that create value for customers

**Key Insight:**
• Value = What customers are willing to pay
• Margin = Value - Cost of activities

**Two Types of Activities:**

**Primary Activities:**
Directly involved in creating/delivering product

**Support Activities:**
Enable primary activities to function

**Goal:** Perform activities better or differently than competitors

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Primary Activities
sections.content:
**"IOOMS" - The flow of product:**

**I**nbound logistics (materials come in)
**O**perations (transform into product)
**O**utbound logistics (product goes out)
**M**arketing & Sales (get customers)
**S**ervice (support after sale)

Think: IN → MAKE → OUT → SELL → SUPPORT

sections.title: Support Activities
sections.content:
**Firm Infrastructure:**
• General management, planning, finance
• Legal, government affairs
• Supports entire chain

**Human Resource Management:**
• Recruiting, training, compensation
• Affects all primary activities

**Technology Development:**
• R&D, process improvement
• Product and process technology

**Procurement:**
• Purchasing inputs for all activities
• Not just materials - also equipment, services

sections.title: Using Value Chain for Cost Advantage
sections.content:
**Steps:**

**1. Identify Value Activities**
• Map all activities
• Separate discrete activities

**2. Assign Costs**
• Activity-based costing approach
• Identify cost drivers

**3. Analyze Cost Behavior**
• What drives cost in each activity?
• Economies of scale? Learning curve?

**4. Identify Linkages**
• How do activities affect each other?
• Trade-offs between activities

**5. Find Improvement Opportunities**
• Reduce costs without reducing value
• Or add value worth more than cost

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Linkages Between Activities
sections.content:
**Activities are interconnected!**

**Example: Quality at Operations**
• Higher quality increases operations cost
• BUT reduces service costs (fewer repairs)
• AND improves marketing (better reputation)

**Trade-off Analysis:**
• Can’t optimize each activity in isolation
• Must consider system-wide effects

**Upstream/Downstream Linkages:**
• Supplier quality affects your operations
• Your product design affects customer's costs

**Key Point:** Competitive advantage often comes from managing linkages better than competitors.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Value chain shows all activities that create customer value
Primary activities: Inbound → Operations → Outbound → Marketing → Service
Support activities: Infrastructure, HR, Technology, Procurement
Margin = Value created - Cost of activities
Analyze costs and value contribution of each activity
Linkages between activities can be source of competitive advantage
Use with ABC to understand true activity costs

knowledgeCheck.question: In Porter's Value Chain framework, which of the following would be classified as a support activity rather than a primary activity?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Employee training and development is part of Human Resource Management, which is a support activity that enables all primary activities. Shipping is Outbound Logistics, Manufacturing is Operations, and Advertising is Marketing & Sales - all primary activities.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Support activities enable primary activities; they don't directly produce or deliver the product.

title: Competitive Intelligence and Market Analysis
description: Gather and analyze competitive information to support strategic decisions

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: You can't make good strategic decisions in a vacuum! CMAs need to understand competitors' cost structures, pricing strategies, and market positions. Competitive intelligence turns external data into actionable insights for budgeting, pricing, and strategic planning.

sections.title: Competitive Intelligence Framework
sections.content:
**Definition:** Systematic gathering and analysis of information about competitors and the competitive environment

**Key Questions to Answer:**
• Who are our competitors?
• What are their strategies?
• What are their strengths/weaknesses?
• How do their costs compare to ours?
• What are they likely to do next?

**Ethical Boundaries:**
• Use publicly available information
• Never misrepresent identity
• Respect confidential information
• Follow legal guidelines

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Porter's Five Forces
sections.content:
**"BREST" - Five Forces in Industry:**

**B**uyers (bargaining power)
**R**ivalry (competitive intensity)
**E**ntry (threat of new entrants)
**S**ubstitutes (threat of alternatives)
**T**rade suppliers (bargaining power)

**Strong forces = Lower industry profitability**
**Weak forces = Higher industry profitability**

sections.title: Competitive Benchmarking
sections.content:
**Process of comparing performance metrics:**

**Types of Benchmarking:**

**1. Competitive Benchmarking**
• Compare to direct competitors
• Same industry, similar products

**2. Functional Benchmarking**
• Compare specific functions
• Best practices from any industry

**3. Internal Benchmarking**
• Compare departments/divisions
• Share best practices within company

**Key Metrics to Benchmark:**
• Cost per unit
• Gross margin
• Inventory turns
• Employee productivity
• Customer acquisition cost

sections.title: Strategic Group Analysis
sections.content:
**Definition:** Cluster competitors with similar strategies

**Strategic Dimensions:**
• Price/quality positioning
• Geographic scope
• Product breadth
• Distribution channels
• Vertical integration

**Example: Retail Industry**
• Group 1: Discount retailers (Walmart, Target)
• Group 2: Premium retailers (Nordstrom)
• Group 3: Specialty (Best Buy, Home Depot)
• Group 4: Online pure-play (Amazon)

**Insight:** Competition is most intense WITHIN strategic groups

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Analysis vs Espionage
sections.content:
**Competitive Intelligence is LEGAL and ETHICAL:**

**Legal Sources:**
• SEC filings, public financial statements
• Trade show observations
• Reverse engineering of products
• Published interviews/speeches

**Illegal/Unethical:**
• Theft of trade secrets
• Bribery of employees
• Electronic surveillance
• Misrepresentation to get info

**The Line:** Would you be embarrassed if your methods were public?

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Competitive intelligence uses public information ethically
Porter's Five Forces assesses industry profitability
Benchmarking compares your performance to competitors or best practices
Strategic groups cluster competitors with similar strategies
Competition is strongest within strategic groups
CMAs use competitive data for pricing, budgeting, and strategy
Always stay within ethical and legal boundaries

knowledgeCheck.question: According to Porter's Five Forces model, which situation would indicate HIGH industry profitability?
options: Powerful buyers who can demand price cuts
knowledgeCheck.explanation: High barriers to entry protect existing firms from new competitors, allowing higher profits. The other options (many substitutes, powerful buyers, intense rivalry) all REDUCE industry profitability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Strong forces reduce profitability; weak forces allow companies to earn higher returns.

title: Enterprise Risk Assessment Frameworks
description: Implement structured approaches to identify, assess, and prioritize organizational risks

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Organizations face countless risks - they can't address them all equally! Risk assessment frameworks help prioritize where to focus attention and resources. CMAs must understand these frameworks to participate in strategic risk discussions.

sections.title: COSO Enterprise Risk Management
sections.content:
**The dominant ERM framework:**

**COSO ERM 2017 Components:**

**1. Governance & Culture**
• Board oversight
• Risk culture

**2. Strategy & Objective-Setting**
• Integrate with strategy
• Risk appetite

**3. Performance**
• Risk identification
• Risk assessment
• Risk prioritization
• Risk response

**4. Review & Revision**
• Assessment of change
• Review of risk

**5. Information, Communication & Reporting**
• Leverage information systems
• Report on risk

sections.title: Risk Assessment Matrix
sections.content:
**Classic Tool: Impact vs Likelihood**

**Risk Score = Impact × Likelihood**

**Scale (example):**
• Impact: 1 (minimal) to 5 (catastrophic)
• Likelihood: 1 (rare) to 5 (almost certain)

**Priority Zones:**
• Score 15-25: Critical - Immediate attention
• Score 8-14: High - Active management
• Score 4-7: Medium - Monitor
• Score 1-3: Low - Accept

**Example:**
Cyber breach: Impact 5, Likelihood 3 = 15 (Critical)
Minor supplier delay: Impact 2, Likelihood 4 = 8 (High)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Risk Response Strategies
sections.content:
**"TARA" - The Four Rs of Risk Response:**

**T**olerate (Accept) - Accept the risk and its consequences
**A**void - Eliminate the risk by not doing the activity
**R**educe (Mitigate) - Implement controls to reduce likelihood/impact
**A**ssign (Transfer) - Transfer to third party (insurance, contracts)

**Match strategy to risk priority!**

sections.title: Heat Map Visualization
sections.content:
**Visual representation of risk matrix:**

**Color Coding:**
• 🟢 Green: Low risk (accept/monitor)
• 🟡 Yellow: Medium risk (controls needed)
• 🟠 Orange: High risk (active management)
• 🔴 Red: Critical risk (immediate action)

**Layout:**
• Y-axis: Impact (low to high, bottom to top)
• X-axis: Likelihood (low to high, left to right)
• Risks plotted based on assessment

**Benefits:**
• Easy to communicate to executives/board
• Visual prioritization
• Shows risk portfolio at a glance

sections.title: Risk Appetite and Tolerance
sections.content:
**Risk Appetite:**
• Broad statement of risk-taking willingness
• Set by board/senior management
• Aligned with strategy
• Example: "We accept moderate risk to achieve growth"

**Risk Tolerance:**
• Specific acceptable deviation from objectives
• Measurable boundaries
• Example: "Maximum 5% revenue loss from single customer"

**Relationship:**
Risk Appetite > Risk Tolerance
(Appetite is the philosophy, tolerance is the specific limit)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Terms to Know
sections.content:
**Don't confuse these:**

**Inherent Risk:**
• Risk BEFORE controls or responses
• Raw exposure

**Residual Risk:**
• Risk AFTER controls implemented
• What remains

**Risk Appetite:**
• How much risk willing to accept
• Strategic level

**Risk Tolerance:**
• Specific acceptable variation
• Operational level

**Exam loves testing the inherent vs residual distinction!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: COSO ERM provides comprehensive risk management framework
Risk score = Impact × Likelihood for prioritization
Heat maps visualize risk portfolio for communication
TARA responses: Tolerate, Avoid, Reduce, Assign
Risk appetite is strategic willingness; tolerance is specific limits
Inherent risk is before controls; residual is after
Match risk response strategy to priority level

knowledgeCheck.question: A company assesses a risk with an impact score of 4 and a likelihood score of 3. After implementing controls, the likelihood drops to 2. What is the residual risk score?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Residual risk is calculated AFTER controls are implemented. Impact (4) × New Likelihood (2) = 8. The inherent risk was 4 × 3 = 12, but controls reduced it to 8.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Use the post-control likelihood to calculate residual risk.

title: Scenario Planning and Strategic Forecasting
description: Develop and use scenarios to prepare for alternative futures

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: The future is uncertain - planning for only one outcome is risky! Scenario planning develops multiple plausible futures so organizations can prepare for various outcomes. CMAs quantify financial implications of different scenarios to support strategic decisions.

sections.title: What Is Scenario Planning?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A disciplined method for imagining possible futures

**Not forecasting - it's different:**
• Forecasting: What WILL happen (single prediction)
• Scenario planning: What COULD happen (multiple possibilities)

**Purpose:**
• Challenge assumptions
• Test strategy robustness
• Identify early warning signs
• Prepare contingency plans

**Famous Example:**
Shell Oil pioneered scenario planning in 1970s
Prepared for oil price shocks before they happened

sections.title: Building Scenarios
sections.content:
**Step-by-Step Process:**

**1. Identify the Focal Question**
• What decision are we informing?
• What time horizon?

**2. Identify Key Drivers**
• What forces will shape the future?
• Economic, technological, regulatory, social

**3. Rank by Impact and Uncertainty**
• Focus on high-impact, high-uncertainty drivers
• Certainties don't need scenarios

**4. Develop Scenario Logics**
• Usually 2-4 distinct scenarios
• Each internally consistent
• Cover range of possibilities

**5. Flesh Out Narratives**
• Rich stories, not just bullet points
• Helps people engage with scenarios

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: 2×2 Matrix
sections.content:
**Common technique: Two drivers, four scenarios**

**Pick two critical uncertainties:**
• Driver 1: Could go high or low
• Driver 2: Could go high or low

**Create matrix:**
```
              Driver 2 High    Driver 2 Low
            +---------------+---------------+
Driver 1    |   Scenario A  |   Scenario B  |
  High      |  (Both high)  |  (Mixed)      |
            +---------------+---------------+
Driver 1    |   Scenario C  |   Scenario D  |
  Low       |   (Mixed)     |  (Both low)   |
            +---------------+---------------+
```

**Each quadrant = distinct scenario to analyze**

sections.title: Financial Modeling for Scenarios
sections.content:
**CMA's Role: Quantify Each Scenario**

**For each scenario, model:**
• Revenue implications
• Cost changes
• Capital requirements
• Cash flow impact
• Key financial ratios

**Analysis Questions:**
• Does our strategy work in all scenarios?
• What triggers would indicate each scenario?
• What hedges or options could we build in?
• Where are we most vulnerable?

**Output:**
• Range of financial outcomes
• Contingency triggers and plans
• Strategic options to preserve

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Scenario Characteristics
sections.content:
**Good scenarios are:**

✓ Plausible (could actually happen)
✓ Internally consistent (logic holds together)
✓ Distinct (not just variations of same theme)
✓ Relevant (matter for the decision)
✓ Challenging (force new thinking)

**Bad scenarios:**
✗ Assign probabilities (that's forecasting)
✗ Predict the future (that's impossible)
✗ Pick a "most likely" (defeats purpose)
✗ Too many (3-4 is optimal)

**Exam Tip:** Scenarios are planning tools, not predictions!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Scenario planning imagines multiple plausible futures
Different from forecasting (which predicts one outcome)
Focus on high-impact, high-uncertainty drivers
2×2 matrix commonly used to create four scenarios
Each scenario should be plausible, consistent, distinct
CMAs model financial implications of each scenario
Good for testing strategy robustness and preparing contingencies

knowledgeCheck.question: A company is using scenario planning and has developed four scenarios for the next decade. Management asks: 'Which scenario should we plan for?' What is the best response?
options: The most likely scenario based on current trends
The worst-case scenario to be safe
All scenarios - develop a strategy robust across all four
Average the scenarios to create a single forecast
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Scenario planning doesn't predict which future will occur. The goal is to develop strategies that work reasonably well across multiple scenarios, identify early warning signals for each, and prepare contingency actions. Picking one scenario defeats the purpose.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Scenario planning is about preparation, not prediction.

title: Mission, Vision, and Corporate Strategy
description: Understand foundational strategic elements that guide organizational direction

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Strategy doesn't start with numbers - it starts with PURPOSE! CMAs must understand how mission, vision, and values translate into strategic objectives and financial targets. Budgets and metrics should align with organizational purpose.

sections.title: Strategic Hierarchy
sections.content:
**From Purpose to Action:**

**Mission (Why We Exist)**
• Core purpose
• What value we create
• Who we serve
↓
**Vision (Where We're Going)**
• Aspirational future state
• Long-term ambition
↓
**Values (How We Operate)**
• Guiding principles
• Behavioral standards
↓
**Strategy (How We Win)**
• Competitive approach
• Key choices
↓
**Objectives & Metrics (What We Track)**
• Specific targets
• KPIs and measures

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Good Statements
sections.content:
**"CRISP" - Effective Mission/Vision:**

**C**lear - Easy to understand
**R**elevant - Meaningful to stakeholders
**I**nspiring - Motivates action
**S**pecific - Defines a focus area
**P**ractical - Can guide decisions

**Test:** Could anyone in the organization use this to make a decision?

sections.title: Corporate Values
sections.content:
**Definition:** Core beliefs that guide behavior

**Purpose:**
• Shape organizational culture
• Guide difficult decisions
• Attract like-minded employees
• Build stakeholder trust

**Examples:**
• Integrity - Do the right thing
• Innovation - Challenge status quo
• Customer Focus - Put customer first
• Excellence - Strive for quality
• Teamwork - Collaborate to win

**Key:** Values must be LIVED, not just displayed
What happens when values conflict with short-term profit?

sections.title: Connecting to Financial Planning
sections.content:
**CMA's Role - Translate Purpose to Numbers:**

**From Mission to Budget:**
Mission emphasizes customer service
→ Strategic objective: Improve satisfaction
→ KPI: Customer satisfaction score
→ Target: 90% by year-end
→ Initiative: Service training program
→ Budget: $500,000 for training

**Red Flag Questions:**
• Does our budget support our mission?
• Do our metrics align with our values?
• Would our vision be achievable with current plans?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Strategy Levels
sections.content:
**Know the three levels:**

**Corporate Strategy:**
• What businesses should we be in?
• Portfolio decisions
• Diversification, acquisitions

**Business (Competitive) Strategy:**
• How do we compete in this market?
• Cost leadership, differentiation, focus

**Functional Strategy:**
• How does each function support business strategy?
• Marketing strategy, operations strategy, etc.

**CMAs work at all levels but especially business and functional

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Mission defines current purpose; Vision defines future aspiration
Values guide behavior and shape culture
Strategic hierarchy flows from purpose to metrics
Good statements are Clear, Relevant, Inspiring, Specific, Practical
Corporate strategy: what businesses; Business strategy: how to compete
CMAs translate strategic intent into budgets and targets
All financial plans should align with organizational purpose

knowledgeCheck.question: Which statement is a VISION statement? "We provide affordable, reliable transportation to urban communities" OR "To be the world's most trusted transportation company by 2030"
options: The first is vision, second is mission
The second is vision, first is mission
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The second statement is a vision - it's future-focused and aspirational ('To be the world's most trusted... by 2030'). The first statement is a mission - it describes what the company currently does and for whom.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Mission describes WHAT you do now; Vision describes WHERE you want to be.

title: SWOT Analysis and Strategic Assessment
description: Apply SWOT framework to evaluate organizational strategic position

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: SWOT is simple but powerful! CMAs use SWOT to translate qualitative strategic insights into quantitative plans. Understanding competitive position helps prioritize investments and anticipate risks in financial forecasts.

sections.title: SWOT Overview
sections.content:
**Four Categories:**

**Internal Factors (Controllable)**
• **S**trengths - What we do well
• **W**eaknesses - What we need to improve

**External Factors (Uncontrollable)**
• **O**pportunities - Favorable external conditions
• **T**hreats - Unfavorable external conditions

**Strategic Logic:**
• Leverage strengths to capture opportunities
• Address weaknesses before threats exploit them
• Use strengths to defend against threats
• Avoid opportunities that expose weaknesses

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SWOT Strategies
sections.content:
**Match factors into strategies:**

**S-O (Strength-Opportunity):**
Aggressive - Use strengths to capture opportunities
"We're good at X, market wants X - GROW!"

**W-O (Weakness-Opportunity):**
Developmental - Fix weaknesses to capture opportunities
"Market wants X, we need to build capability"

**S-T (Strength-Threat):**
Defensive - Use strengths to block threats
"Competitor entering, leverage our brand loyalty"

**W-T (Weakness-Threat):**
Survival - Minimize damage
"We're exposed, need to divest or partner"

sections.title: SWOT Analysis Example
sections.content:
**Regional Bank Analysis:**

**Strengths:**
• Strong local brand recognition
• Personal customer relationships
• Low cost deposit base
