• Deep community ties

**Weaknesses:**
• Limited digital capabilities
• Small IT budget vs nationals
• No investment services

**Opportunities:**
• Growing local economy
• Small business lending demand
• Fintech partnership options

**Threats:**
• National bank expansion
• Digital-only competitors
• Interest rate pressure

**Strategy implication:**
Partner with fintech (W-O) to compete digitally
while leveraging relationships (S-T)

sections.title: SWOT Best Practices
sections.content:
**Do:**
• Be specific - "30% faster delivery" not "good logistics"
• Prioritize - Top 3-5 items per category
• Validate - Get data to support claims
• Compare to competitors - Relative, not absolute
• Update regularly - Environment changes

**Don't:**
• List everything - Focus on strategic factors
• Confuse internal/external - Customers aren't strengths
• Ignore connections - Look for patterns across quadrants
• Stop at listing - Turn into actions

**CMA Role:**
• Quantify strengths (market share %, cost advantage)
• Estimate opportunity value
• Model threat scenarios in forecasts

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: SWOT Limitations
sections.content:
**Know SWOT weaknesses:**

**Static snapshot:**
• Markets change rapidly
• Needs regular updating

**Subjective:**
• What's a strength vs weakness?
• Different perspectives disagree

**No prioritization:**
• Equal weight to all factors
• Doesn't show which matter most

**No implementation guidance:**
• Lists factors, not solutions
• Requires additional frameworks

**Complement SWOT with:**
• Porter's Five Forces (industry)
• PESTEL (macro environment)
• Value chain (internal processes)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SWOT: Strengths, Weaknesses (internal) + Opportunities, Threats (external)
Internal factors are controllable; External factors must be adapted to
Four strategies: S-O (grow), W-O (develop), S-T (defend), W-T (survive)
Be specific and prioritize top factors
CMAs quantify and model SWOT factors financially
Limitations: Static, subjective, no prioritization
Complement with Porter's Five Forces, PESTEL, Value Chain

knowledgeCheck.question: A company has strong R&D capabilities (strength) and sees growing demand for sustainable products (opportunity). Which strategic approach should they use?
options: W-T: Minimize exposure to changing market
S-O: Leverage R&D to develop sustainable product line
W-O: Acquire a company with sustainability expertise
S-T: Use R&D to defend current product portfolio
knowledgeCheck.explanation: With a Strength (R&D) matching an Opportunity (sustainability demand), the S-O approach applies: aggressively use the existing strength to capture the market opportunity. This is the 'grow' strategy.
knowledgeCheck.hint: S-O strategy combines internal strengths with external opportunities.

title: Value Chain Analysis
description: Identify competitive advantage through value chain mapping and optimization

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Where does your company CREATE value? CMAs use value chain analysis to identify cost advantages, differentiation opportunities, and outsourcing candidates. This connects strategy to cost accounting and process improvement.

sections.title: Porter's Value Chain Model
sections.content:
**Primary Activities (direct value creation):**

**Inbound Logistics:**
• Receiving, storing, distributing inputs
• Material handling, warehousing

**Operations:**
• Transforming inputs to outputs
• Manufacturing, assembly

**Outbound Logistics:**
• Delivering products to customers
• Distribution, delivery

**Marketing & Sales:**
• Persuading customers to buy
• Advertising, pricing, channels

**Service:**
• Post-sale support
• Installation, repair, training

sections.title: Support Activities
sections.content:
**Firm Infrastructure:**
• General management
• Finance, accounting, legal
• Strategic planning

**Human Resource Management:**
• Recruiting, training, development
• Compensation systems

**Technology Development:**
• R&D, product design
• Process innovation
• IT systems

**Procurement:**
• Purchasing inputs
• Supplier relationships
• Supports all primary activities

**Key Insight:**
Support activities enable and enhance primary activities

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Value Chain Analysis Steps
sections.content:
**"MCLCB" - Map, Cost, Link, Compare, Build:**

**M**ap activities - Identify all value-creating activities

**C**ost activities - Assign costs to each activity

**L**ink activities - Find connections and dependencies

**C**ompare - Benchmark against competitors

**B**uild advantage - Strengthen or outsource

**Goal:** Find where you can:
• Perform activity at LOWER COST
• Perform activity DIFFERENTLY (differentiate)

sections.title: Value Chain Analysis Example
sections.content:
**Online Retailer Analysis:**

**Competitive Advantage in:**
• Inbound Logistics - Efficient distribution centers
• Technology - Recommendation algorithms
• Outbound - Last-mile delivery network

**Neutral:**
• Marketing - Comparable to competitors
• HR - Standard practices

**Weakness:**
• Service - Customer complaints about returns

**Strategic Implication:**
• Invest more in logistics (strength)
• Fix returns process (weakness)
• Don't over-invest in marketing (neutral)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Value Chain vs Supply Chain
sections.content:
**Different Concepts:**

**Value Chain:**
• Internal analysis of ONE company
• Where do WE add value?
• Competitive advantage focus
• Porter's framework

**Supply Chain:**
• External view across MULTIPLE companies
• Flow of materials and information
• Coordination and efficiency focus
• From raw material to end customer

**Sometimes combined:**
"Value Network" or "Industry Value Chain"
Analyzes value creation across all players
• Supplier's margin + Your margin + Buyer's margin

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Value chain: Primary activities + Support activities
Primary: Inbound, Operations, Outbound, Marketing, Service
Support: Infrastructure, HR, Technology, Procurement
Use to identify sources of cost advantage or differentiation
Compare activities to competitors (benchmarking)
Outsource activities where others have advantage
Value chain is internal; Supply chain spans organizations

knowledgeCheck.question: A pharmaceutical company's clinical trial management and drug formulation belong to which value chain activity?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Clinical trials and drug formulation are R&D activities, which fall under Technology Development - a support activity. Operations would be the actual manufacturing of approved drugs.
knowledgeCheck.hint: R&D activities that create new products are support activities under Technology Development.

title: Competitive Dynamics and Game Theory Basics
description: Apply game theory concepts to strategic decision-making and competitor analysis

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Business isn't played in isolation! Your competitors respond to your moves, and you must anticipate their reactions. Game theory provides frameworks for strategic thinking when outcomes depend on others' choices.

sections.title: Game Theory Basics
sections.content:
**What is Game Theory?**
• Study of strategic interaction
• When your payoff depends on others' choices
• Applicable to competitors, suppliers, regulators

**Key Concepts:**

**Players:** Decision-makers in the game

**Strategies:** Choices available to each player

**Payoffs:** Outcomes for each strategy combination

**Nash Equilibrium:**
No player can improve by unilaterally changing strategy
Stable outcome - nobody wants to deviate

sections.title: The Prisoner's Dilemma
sections.content:
**Classic Example:**
Two suspects, separate interrogation

| | B Cooperates | B Defects |
|---|-------------|-----------|
| A Cooperates | Both: 1 year | A: 10 yrs, B: 0 |
| A Defects | A: 0, B: 10 yrs | Both: 5 years |

**Dominant Strategy:** Defect (for each player)
**Nash Equilibrium:** Both defect (5 years each)
**Paradox:** Mutual cooperation (1 year each) is better!

**Business Application:**
Price wars, advertising battles
Both companies would benefit from restraint
But individually each has incentive to compete

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Strategic Scenarios
sections.content:
**When to apply game theory thinking:**

**"PRICE" situations:**

**P**rice wars - Will competitor match my cut?

**R**esponsive reactions - How will they counter?

**I**ndustry consolidation - Merger implications?

**C**apacity decisions - If we expand, will they?

**E**ntry/exit - Will they fight our entry?

**Key Question:**
What will competitors do, and how does that change my best choice?

sections.title: Strategic Implications
sections.content:
**Commitment:**
• Credible commitment changes game
• Example: Building extra capacity
• Signals you'll fight entry

**Reputation:**
• Past behavior shapes expectations
• Price wars may deter future competition
• Cost vs. benefit of "toughness"

**Communication:**
• Signals and announcements matter
• Public price increases
• Investment announcements
• (Careful: Antitrust concerns)

**Timing:**
• First-mover advantages and disadvantages
• Sometimes waiting is strategic
• Learn from others' mistakes

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Dominant Strategy vs Equilibrium
sections.content:
**Different Concepts:**

**Dominant Strategy:**
• Best regardless of what others do
• Not all games have one
• If exists, rational player always chooses it

**Nash Equilibrium:**
• Stable outcome - no one wants to unilaterally change
• Every game has at least one (may be mixed)
• Doesn't require dominant strategies

**Prisoner's Dilemma:**
• Defect IS dominant strategy for each
• (Both defect) IS Nash equilibrium
• But it's not OPTIMAL for the group!

**Not all Nash equilibria are efficient!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Game theory analyzes strategic interaction where outcomes depend on others
Nash equilibrium: No one benefits from unilateral change
Prisoner's dilemma: Individual rationality leads to collective irrationality
Zero-sum: One's gain is other's loss; Positive-sum: Both can win
Commitment, reputation, communication, and timing are strategic tools
Dominant strategy: Best regardless of others' choices
Apply to pricing, capacity, entry decisions with competitor response

knowledgeCheck.question: Two airlines have been tacitly maintaining high fares on a route. One airline is considering a price cut. This scenario most resembles:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: This is a classic prisoner's dilemma. Both benefit from cooperation (high fares = high margins), but each has individual incentive to defect (cut price to steal share). If both cut prices, they're both worse off than maintaining high fares.
knowledgeCheck.hint: When both parties benefit from cooperation but each has temptation to 'cheat,' what game is this?

title: Corporate Governance
description: Understand governance structures, board responsibilities, and accountability mechanisms

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Governance failures destroy companies (Enron, WorldCom, Wirecard)! CMAs work within governance structures and support boards with accurate financial information. Understanding governance is essential for ethics, controls, and career advancement.

sections.title: What is Corporate Governance?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
System of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled

**Key Players:**
• Shareholders (owners)
• Board of Directors (oversight)
• Management (operations)
• External auditors (independent assurance)
• Regulators (legal compliance)

**Purpose:**
• Protect shareholder interests
• Ensure accountability
• Promote transparency
• Reduce agency costs
• Build stakeholder trust

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Key Board Committees
sections.content:
**"ACN" - Three critical committees:**

**A**udit Committee
• Oversees financial reporting
• Hires external auditors
• Reviews internal controls
• Must be independent directors
• Financial expert required

**C**ompensation Committee
• Sets executive pay
• Aligns incentives with performance
• Approves equity grants
• Should be independent

**N**ominating/Governance Committee
• Selects board candidates
• Succession planning
• Governance policies
• Board evaluation

sections.title: Director Independence
sections.content:
**Why Independence Matters:**
• Objective oversight of management
• Protect minority shareholders
• Challenge management decisions

**Independence Criteria:**
• Not current/recent employee
• No material business relationship
• Not a relative of executives
• No cross-board relationships

**NYSE/NASDAQ Requirements:**
• Majority of board must be independent
• Audit committee: 100% independent
• Compensation committee: 100% independent

**Lead Independent Director:**
• Chairs independent sessions
• Counterbalance if CEO is Chair

sections.title: Governance Best Practices
sections.content:
**Board Structure:**
• Separate CEO and Chair roles
• Regular executive sessions (no management)
• Director term limits
• Board diversity

**Transparency:**
• Clear disclosure policies
• Investor communication
• Proxy statement clarity

**Accountability:**
• Director elections
• Say-on-pay votes
• Clawback policies

**Risk Management:**
• Enterprise risk oversight
• Cybersecurity attention
• Crisis preparedness

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Agency Problem
sections.content:
**Managers may act in THEIR interest, not shareholders'!**

**Agency Costs:**
• Excessive perks
• Empire building
• Short-term focus
• Risk aversion
• Entrenchment

**Governance Solutions:**
• Independent board oversight
• Performance-based compensation
• Stock ownership requirements
• External audit
• Threat of takeover

**Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX):**
• CEO/CFO certification
• Internal control attestation
• Audit committee independence
• Whistleblower protection
• Criminal penalties for fraud

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Governance: System directing and controlling companies
Board oversees strategy, CEO, risk, and compliance
Key committees: Audit, Compensation, Nominating
Independence prevents management conflicts
Agency problem: Managers may pursue own interests
SOX: CEO/CFO certification, internal control attestation
Good governance builds trust and reduces cost of capital

knowledgeCheck.question: Which board committee must be composed entirely of independent directors and include at least one financial expert?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Audit Committee must be 100% independent directors under SEC/NYSE rules, and SOX requires at least one 'audit committee financial expert.' This ensures objective oversight of financial reporting and the external audit.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which committee oversees financial reporting and works with external auditors?

title: Ethics in Management Accounting
description: Apply ethical principles and IMA ethical standards in professional practice

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Ethics isn't just about passing the exam - it's about protecting your career and your organization! CMAs face pressure to manipulate numbers, cut corners, and look the other way. The IMA ethical standards provide a framework for navigating these pressures.

sections.title: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice
sections.content:
**Four Ethical Principles:**

**1. Honesty**
• Be truthful in all communications

**2. Fairness**
• Treat all parties fairly

**3. Objectivity**
• Disclose relevant information

**4. Responsibility**
• Act in legitimate interest of organization

**Committed to ethical professional practice**

These principles underlie the four standards that CMAs must follow.

sections.title: The Four Ethical Standards
rows: Maintain expertise, provide accurate info, recognize limitations
Keep info confidential, don't use for personal gain
Avoid conflicts, refuse gifts that impair judgment, abstain when conflicted
Communicate fairly, disclose all relevant info, disclose limitations

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CCIC Standards
sections.content:
**Remember: "CCIC" - The Four Standards:**

**C**ompetence
• Know your stuff
• Stay current (CPE)
• Don't exceed your expertise

**C**onfidentiality
• Protect sensitive information
• No insider trading
• Don't use for personal benefit

**I**ntegrity
• Be ethical in actions
• Avoid conflicts of interest
• Refuse inappropriate gifts

**C**redibility
• Be accurate and complete
• Disclose all relevant info
• Acknowledge limitations

**Exam Tip:** Know specific requirements under each!

sections.title: Resolution of Ethical Conflicts
sections.content:
**IMA's Recommended Process:**

**1. Discuss with immediate superior**
• Unless they're involved
• Document the discussion

**2. If unresolved, go up the chain**
• Next level of management
• Audit committee if needed

**3. Clarify issues with objective advisor**
• IMA ethics hotline
• Legal counsel
• Don't go it alone

**4. Consult your own attorney**
• Understand your rights
• Document everything

**5. As a last resort...**
• Resignation may be necessary
• External reporting (whistleblowing)

sections.title: Common Ethical Dilemmas
sections.content:
**Earnings Management:**
• Pressure to meet targets
• "Just this quarter"
• Channel stuffing, cookie jars

**Conflicts of Interest:**
• Personal relationships with vendors
• Outside business interests
• Gift acceptance

**Confidentiality:**
• Information sharing
• Job interviews
• Social situations

**Pressure from Above:**
• "Make the numbers work"
• Suppress bad news
• Retaliation fears

**Key Insight:**
Most ethical failures start small and escalate

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Confidentiality Exceptions
sections.content:
**When CAN you disclose confidential information?**

**Legal Obligation:**
• Subpoenas
• Legal proceedings
• Regulatory requirements

**Professional Obligation:**
• Quality review by professional body
• Successor accountant inquiry

**Never OK:**
• Personal financial gain
• Casual disclosure
• Competitive intelligence
• Without authorization

**Gray Areas:**
• Whistleblowing on fraud
• Get legal advice first
• Understand protections

**Default: Keep it confidential unless required by law

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four principles: Honesty, Fairness, Objectivity, Responsibility
Four standards: Competence, Confidentiality, Integrity, Credibility (CCIC)
Conflicts: Start with supervisor, escalate if needed
Use IMA ethics hotline for guidance
Document ethical concerns and discussions
Confidentiality: Hold unless legally required to disclose
Resignation may be necessary as last resort

knowledgeCheck.question: A CMA discovers their company is manipulating revenue recognition to meet analyst expectations. They should first:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: IMA guidelines recommend starting by discussing ethical concerns with your immediate supervisor (unless they're involved in the issue). This gives the organization a chance to address the problem internally. Escalation, external reporting, or resignation are later steps if the issue isn't resolved.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The IMA conflict resolution process starts internally.

title: Industry Analysis
description: Analyze industry structure and competitive dynamics using strategic frameworks

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Industry structure determines profitability potential! CMAs support strategic planning with industry analysis - understanding competitive forces, industry trends, and structural changes. This context is essential for budgeting, forecasting, and strategic decisions.

sections.title: Porter's Five Forces
sections.content:
**Industry Attractiveness Depends On:**

**1. Rivalry Among Existing Competitors**
• Number and size of competitors
• Industry growth rate
• Fixed costs and exit barriers

**2. Threat of New Entrants**
• Barriers to entry
• Capital requirements
• Expected retaliation

**3. Threat of Substitutes**
• Alternative solutions
• Price-performance trade-off

**4. Bargaining Power of Buyers**
• Buyer concentration
• Switching costs

**5. Bargaining Power of Suppliers**
• Supplier concentration
• Input differentiation

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Entry Barriers
sections.content:
**"SCALE" - Barriers to New Entrants:**

**S**cale economies
• Minimum efficient scale
• Cost disadvantage for small entrants

**C**apital requirements
• Investment needed
• Sunk costs

**A**ccess barriers
• Distribution channels
• Supplier relationships
• Customer access

**L**egal/regulatory
• Patents, licenses
• Government policy

**E**xperience advantages
• Learning curve
• Know-how
• Brand recognition

**High barriers = Protected industry**

sections.title: Industry Life Cycle
sections.content:
**Introduction:**
• New product/service
• High uncertainty
• Few competitors
• Losses common

**Growth:**
• Rapid market expansion
• New entrants attracted
• Focus on market share
• Profits improve

**Maturity:**
• Growth slows
• Intense competition
• Focus on efficiency
• Consolidation

**Decline:**
• Falling demand
• Exit by weaker players
• Harvest or divest
• Cost control critical

sections.title: Strategic Implications
sections.content:
**For CMAs:**

**Growth Industry:**
• Invest in capacity
• Flexible budgets for expansion
• Focus on market share metrics

**Mature Industry:**
• Tight cost control
• Efficiency metrics
• Working capital optimization
• Cash cow management

**Declining Industry:**
• Asset write-downs
• Exit cost analysis
• Cash flow focus
• Harvest strategy

**Industry Analysis Informs:**
• Revenue forecasts
• Capital budgets
• Competitive benchmarks
• Strategic assumptions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Static vs Dynamic
sections.content:
**Five Forces is a snapshot - industries change!**

**Disruption Factors:**
• Technology changes
• Regulation shifts
• Globalization
• Customer preferences

**Examples:**
• Streaming disrupted video rental
• Mobile disrupted cameras
• eCommerce disrupted retail

**Update analysis regularly:**
• Annual strategic planning
• When major changes occur
• Monitor weak signals

**Don't assume current structure is permanent!**

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Five Forces: Rivalry, Entrants, Substitutes, Buyers, Suppliers
High forces = Less attractive industry, lower profits
Entry barriers: Scale, Capital, Access, Legal, Experience
Life Cycle: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline
Stage determines strategic and financial focus
Industry analysis informs budgets and forecasts
Update analysis regularly - industries change

knowledgeCheck.question: An industry has four large customers who purchase 80% of output. This represents high power for which Five Forces element?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Concentrated buyers (few large customers) means high bargaining power of buyers. They can demand lower prices, better terms, and higher quality. This puts pressure on industry profitability as suppliers must compete for their business.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Who controls the purchasing decisions in this scenario?

title: Business Ethics Frameworks
description: Apply ethical frameworks to business decision-making

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Ethics isn't just about compliance - it's core to the profession! CMAs face ethical dilemmas involving financial reporting, resource allocation, and confidentiality. Understanding ethical frameworks helps make and defend difficult decisions. Ethics is a significant portion of the CMA exam.

sections.title: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice
sections.content:
**Four Principles:**

**1. Honesty:**
• Truthfulness and integrity
• No deception

**2. Fairness:**
• Impartial treatment
• Consider all stakeholders

**3. Objectivity:**
• Unbiased analysis
• Disclose conflicts of interest

**4. Responsibility:**
• Accountability for actions
• Serve public interest

**Four Standards:**
• Competence
• Confidentiality
• Integrity
• Credibility

sections.title: IMA Standards Detail
rows: Avoid conflicts, refuse gifts that influence

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Ethical Frameworks
sections.content:
**Major Ethical Theories:**

**Utilitarianism (Consequences):**
• Greatest good for greatest number
• Weigh costs and benefits
• Outcome focused

**Deontology (Duty/Rules):**
• Follow moral rules regardless of outcome
• Kant: Act as if making universal law
• Rights-based thinking

**Virtue Ethics (Character):**
• What would a virtuous person do?
• Develop good character traits
• Courage, honesty, fairness

**Justice/Fairness:**
• Distributional fairness
• Procedural fairness
• Equal treatment

sections.title: Ethical Decision-Making Process
sections.content:
**Step 1: Identify the Issue**
• What is the ethical dilemma?
• Who are affected stakeholders?

**Step 2: Analyze Facts**
• Gather relevant information
• Identify applicable standards

**Step 3: Consider Alternatives**
• Generate options
• Apply ethical frameworks

**Step 4: Evaluate Options**
• Consider consequences
• Apply rules/duties
• What would stakeholders think?

**Step 5: Decide and Act**
• Choose best course
• Be prepared to defend

**Step 6: Reflect**
• Review outcome
• Learn for future

sections.title: Resolving Ethical Conflicts (IMA)
sections.content:
**When Facing Ethical Conflict:**

**1. Follow Organization Policies**
• Check if policy addresses situation
• Use internal reporting channels

**2. Discuss with Supervisor**
• Unless supervisor is involved
• Seek clarification

**3. Escalate as Needed**
• Next level of management
• Board or audit committee if necessary

**4. Seek Outside Advice**
• IMA ethics helpline
• Attorney (if needed)
• Keep confidential information protected

**Last Resort:**
• Consider resignation if unresolvable
• Document everything

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Ethics Questions
sections.content:
**Common Ethics Exam Scenarios:**

**Pressure Situations:**
• Boss asks to misstate financials
• Pressure to make budget targets
• 'Just this once' rationalization

**Conflicts of Interest:**
• Personal gain from decisions
• Family/friend relationships
• Outside employment

**Confidentiality:**
• Appropriate vs inappropriate sharing
• Leaving for competitor

**Key Answer Patterns:**
• DO: Escalate, document, follow policies
• DON'T: Go to media, ignore, participate
• Consult IMA ethics helpline
• Consider resignation as LAST resort

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IMA Principles: Honesty, Fairness, Objectivity, Responsibility
IMA Standards: Competence, Confidentiality, Integrity, Credibility
Utilitarianism: Greatest good for greatest number
Deontology: Follow rules/duties regardless of outcome
Virtue ethics: What would a person of good character do?
Resolution: Internal policies → Supervisor → Escalate → IMA helpline
Resignation is last resort, not first response

knowledgeCheck.question: A CMA discovers their supervisor is manipulating expense reports. Per IMA guidance, what should be the CMA's first step?
options: Follow the organization's established policies for reporting
Contact the media to expose wrongdoing
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Per IMA guidance, the first step is to follow established organizational policies for handling such situations. This typically means using internal channels. External reporting or resignation comes only after internal options are exhausted, and going to media is never recommended.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Start with internal processes per IMA guidance.

title: Internal Controls Framework
description: Design and evaluate internal controls using COSO framework

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Controls protect the organization and enable reliable reporting! CMAs design, implement, and monitor internal controls over financial processes. COSO framework is THE standard for internal control - understand it thoroughly for the exam and practice.

sections.title: What are Internal Controls?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Processes to provide reasonable assurance regarding:
• Effectiveness and efficiency of operations
• Reliability of financial reporting
• Compliance with laws and regulations

**Who's Responsible:**
• Board of Directors - Oversight
• Management - Implementation
• Internal Audit - Evaluation
• Everyone - Adherence

**Limitations:**
• Collusion
• Management override
• Cost-benefit
• Human error

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Control Activities
sections.content:
**Types of Control Activities:**

**Preventive Controls:**
• Stop errors before they occur
• Approval requirements
• Segregation of duties
• Access restrictions

**Detective Controls:**
• Find errors that occurred
• Reconciliations
• Variance analysis
• Physical counts

**CRAVE-IT Mnemonic:**
• **C**omparisons/Reconciliations
• **R**estripted access
• **A**pprovals/Authorizations
• **V**erifications
• **E**xception reports
• **I**T controls
• **T**ask segregation

sections.title: Segregation of Duties
sections.content:
**Fundamental Control Principle:**
No one person should control all phases of a transaction

**Separate These Functions:**
• **Authorization:** Approve transactions
• **Custody:** Handle assets
• **Recording:** Enter in records
• **Reconciliation:** Verify accuracy

**Example - Purchasing:**
• Person A: Requests purchase
• Person B: Approves purchase
• Person C: Receives goods
• Person D: Records payable
• Person E: Pays invoice

**Small Company Challenge:**
• Compensating controls needed
• Management oversight
• Independent review

sections.title: IT Controls
sections.content:
**General IT Controls:**
• Access security
• Change management
• Computer operations
• Backup and recovery

**Application Controls:**
• Input controls (validation)
• Processing controls (accuracy)
• Output controls (distribution)

**Key Concepts:**
• Passwords and authentication
• User access reviews
• Audit trails
• Data encryption

**IT Risks:**
• System downtime
• Data breach
• Unauthorized changes
• Loss of data

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Control Limitations
sections.content:
**Controls Provide REASONABLE Assurance, Not Absolute!**

**Inherent Limitations:**

**1. Collusion:**
• Multiple people conspiring
• Defeats segregation of duties

**2. Management Override:**
• Those in charge can bypass controls
• Major fraud risk

**3. Cost-Benefit:**
• Controls must be worth their cost
• Perfect control is too expensive

**4. Human Error:**
• Mistakes happen
• Judgment failures

**Key Point:**
Controls REDUCE risk, don't eliminate it
Focus on material risks

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: COSO: Control Environment, Risk Assessment, Control Activities, Info/Comm, Monitoring
Control Environment: Foundation - tone at the top
Preventive controls: Stop errors; Detective: Find errors
Segregation: Authorization, Custody, Recording, Reconciliation
IT Controls: General (access, changes) and Application (input, process, output)
Reasonable assurance: Not absolute; cost-benefit considered
Limitations: Collusion, management override, cost, human error

knowledgeCheck.question: Which COSO component includes the organization's commitment to integrity, ethical values, and competence of its people?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Control Environment is the foundation of internal control and includes integrity, ethical values, management philosophy, organizational structure, HR policies, and assignment of authority. It sets the 'tone at the top' that influences control consciousness.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which component represents the foundation and 'tone at the top'?

title: Corporate Governance Principles
description: Understand governance structures and board responsibilities

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Good governance protects shareholders and ensures accountability! CMAs interact with boards, audit committees, and senior leadership. Understanding governance structures helps CMAs navigate reporting responsibilities and support proper controls. This topic appears frequently on CMA Part 2.

sections.title: Corporate Governance Defined
sections.content:
**What is Corporate Governance?**
• System of rules, practices, processes
• Directs and controls organization
• Balances stakeholder interests
• Ensures accountability

**Key Stakeholders:**
• **Shareholders:** Owners, voting rights
• **Board:** Oversight, strategic direction
• **Management:** Day-to-day operations
• **Employees, Customers, Community**

**Agency Problem:**
Managers (agents) may not act in owners' (principals') best interest
Governance mechanisms address this

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Board Committees
sections.content:
**Three Key Board Committees:**

**Audit Committee:**
• Oversee financial reporting
• Select external auditors
• Review internal controls
• Approve internal audit plan
• Must be independent

**Compensation Committee:**
• Set executive pay
• Approve incentive plans
• Review performance
• Address pay vs performance

**Nominating/Governance Committee:**
• Identify board candidates
• Board composition
• Governance policies
• Board evaluations

**Key Rule:**
All three should be INDEPENDENT directors

sections.title: Board Independence
sections.content:
**Inside Directors:**
• Company executives
• CEO typically on board
• Day-to-day knowledge
• Potential conflicts

**Outside/Independent Directors:**
• No material relationship with company
• Not employees or executives
• Objective oversight
• Required for key committees

**Best Practice:**
• Majority of board = independent
• Separate Chair and CEO roles
• Lead independent director
• Executive sessions without management

sections.title: Sarbanes-Oxley Requirements
sections.content:
**SOX Key Provisions:**
• **Section 302:** CEO/CFO certify financials
• **Section 404:** Internal control assessment
• **Section 906:** Criminal penalties for false certification

**Audit Committee Requirements:**
• All members independent
• At least one financial expert
• Direct responsibility for external auditors
• Procedures for complaints/whistleblowers

**Prohibitions:**
• Loans to executives
• Insider trading during blackout periods
• Auditor conflicts of interest

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Governance Concepts
sections.content:
**Common Confusions:**

**Board vs Management:**
• Board: OVERSIGHT (strategy, monitoring)
• Management: EXECUTION (operations)
• Board should NOT manage!

**Audit Committee Responsibilities:**
• Oversee external auditors (select, evaluate)
• Review internal controls
• NOT manage internal audit
• Internal audit reports TO audit committee

**Independence:**
• Former employee: May need 'cooling off' period
• Family relationships: May impair independence
• Business relationships: Material = not independent

**Fiduciary Duty:**
• Duty of care: Informed decisions
• Duty of loyalty: Act in company's interest

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Governance: System to direct and control organization
Agency problem: Managers may not act for shareholders
Board: Oversight and strategic direction
Key committees: Audit, Compensation, Nominating
All key committees should be independent
SOX: CEO/CFO certification, Section 404 controls
Audit committee: Oversee auditors, financial reporting, controls

knowledgeCheck.question: Which board committee is primarily responsible for overseeing the external audit process?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Audit Committee is responsible for overseeing the external audit process, including selecting auditors, reviewing audit scope, and approving audit fees. This committee must be comprised entirely of independent directors.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Which committee deals with financial reporting and auditors?

title: Fraud Risk and Prevention
description: Identify and prevent occupational fraud

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Fraud costs organizations 5% of revenue annually! CMAs are on the front line of fraud prevention through controls, analysis, and ethical leadership. Understanding fraud schemes and prevention techniques is essential for protecting organizational assets and reputation.

sections.title: Fraud Classification
sections.content:
**Occupational Fraud Categories:**

**Asset Misappropriation:**
• Theft of cash or assets
• Most common (85%+ of cases)
• Smallest median loss
• Examples: Skimming, theft, fraudulent disbursements

**Corruption:**
• Employee misuses influence
• Bribery, kickbacks
• Conflicts of interest
• Moderate frequency and loss

**Financial Statement Fraud:**
• Intentional misstatement
• Least common (<10%)
• Largest median loss
• Examples: Fictitious revenue, hidden liabilities

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Fraud Triangle
sections.content:
**Three Elements (ALL required):**

**1. Pressure (Incentive):**
• Financial problems
• Lifestyle beyond means
• Addiction, gambling
• Performance pressure

**2. Opportunity:**
• Weak internal controls
• Lack of oversight
• Management override
• Access to assets

**3. Rationalization:**
• 'I deserve it'
• 'I'll pay it back'
• 'Everyone does it'
• 'Company can afford it'

**Prevention Focus:**
Reduce OPPORTUNITY (controls)
Harder to change pressure/rationalization

sections.title: Fraud Red Flags
sections.content:
**Behavioral:**
• Living beyond means
• Financial difficulties
• Unusually close vendor relationships
• Refusal to take vacation
• Excessive control issues

**Organizational:**
• Override of controls by management
• Domineering management style
• High turnover in key positions
• Inadequate segregation of duties
• Lack of independent checks

**Transactional:**
• Missing documents
• Unusual journal entries
• Round number transactions
• Transactions just below authorization limits
• Unusually high expense reports

sections.title: Fraud Prevention
sections.content:
**Control Environment:**
• Tone at the top
• Code of ethics
• Clear policies
• Ethical culture

**Internal Controls:**
• Segregation of duties
• Authorization limits
• Physical safeguards
• Independent reconciliation

**Detection Activities:**
• Surprise audits
• Hotline/whistleblower program
• Data analytics
• Continuous monitoring

**Key Statistic:**
Tips are the #1 detection method (~40% of cases)

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Fraud Diamond
sections.content:
**Fraud Diamond (Extension):**

Adds 4th element: **Capability**

• Position in organization
• Intelligence and creativity
• Ego and confidence
• Ability to handle stress
• Coercion skills

**Why It Matters:**
Not everyone with pressure, opportunity, and rationalization commits fraud
They must also have the CAPABILITY

**CMA Role:**
• Design preventive controls
• Support hotline programs
• Conduct data analytics
• Maintain ethical standards
• Report suspicious activity

**Remember:**
CMAs have obligation under IMA ethics to report fraud

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Three fraud types: Asset misappropriation, corruption, financial statement
Asset misappropriation most common, F/S fraud largest loss
Fraud triangle: Pressure, Opportunity, Rationalization
Fraud diamond adds: Capability
Focus prevention on reducing opportunity (controls)
Tips are #1 detection method - support hotlines
Behavioral red flags often precede fraud detection

knowledgeCheck.question: The fraud triangle includes pressure, opportunity, and:
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The fraud triangle consists of Pressure (incentive to commit fraud), Opportunity (weak controls allow fraud), and Rationalization (justification for the behavior). Capability is the fourth element in the fraud diamond, an extension of the triangle.
knowledgeCheck.hint: The triangle has three elements - which one is about justifying the behavior?

title: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice
description: Apply IMA ethical standards in practice

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Ethics is the foundation of the CMA profession! The IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice is THE guiding document for CMAs worldwide. Understanding and applying these standards is essential for maintaining professional integrity. This is heavily tested on the CMA exam.

sections.title: IMA Ethical Principles
sections.content:
**Overarching Principles:**
• **Honesty:** Tell the truth
• **Fairness:** Be impartial
• **Objectivity:** Free from bias
• **Responsibility:** Act responsibly

**These Principles Guide:**
• Decision-making
• Professional conduct
• Relationships
• Communications

**Four Standards:**
• Competence
• Confidentiality
• Integrity
• Credibility

sections.title: IMA Ethical Standards
rows: Maintain expertise, perform duties competently, provide decision-support information
Keep information confidential, don't use for personal advantage
Avoid conflicts, refuse gifts that influence, recognize limitations
Communicate fairly and objectively, disclose all relevant information

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The Four C's (Actually CCIC)
sections.content:
**Competence:**
• Maintain professional competence
• Comply with laws and regulations
• Provide accurate information

**Confidentiality:**
• Keep information confidential
• Don't use for personal advantage
• Ensure subordinates maintain

**Integrity:**
• Mitigate conflicts of interest
• Refrain from activities that prejudice duties
• Refuse gifts that could influence

**Credibility:**
• Communicate information fairly
• Disclose delays/deficiencies
• Disclose ALL relevant information

**Memory:**
'CMAs are CCIC - Competent, Confidential, Integral, Credible'

sections.title: Resolving Ethical Conflicts
sections.content:
**IMA Recommended Process:**

**1. Follow Established Policies:**
• Review company's ethics policy
• Use internal reporting channels

**2. Discuss with Supervisor:**
• Unless supervisor is involved
• Present facts objectively

**3. Escalate if Needed:**
• Go to next level of management
• Use audit committee if available
• Contact ethics hotline

**4. If Unresolved:**
• Consult IMA Ethics Counselor
• Consult attorney
• Consider resignation as last resort

**Note:** Resignation doesn't relieve confidentiality obligations

sections.title: Common Ethical Scenarios
sections.content:
**Pressure to Misstate:**
• Refuse to comply
• Document the request
• Escalate through proper channels

**Confidential Information:**
• Don't share with unauthorized parties
• Don't use for personal benefit
• Maintain after leaving company

**Conflict of Interest:**
• Disclose the conflict
• Recuse from decision-making
• Document resolution

**Gifts and Entertainment:**
• Decline if could influence judgment
• Follow company policy
• Consider appearance

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Ethics Application
sections.content:
**Common Exam Scenarios:**

**What Standard Applies?**
• Failing to disclose all relevant info: Credibility
• Using insider info for trading: Confidentiality + Integrity
• Presenting biased analysis: Credibility
• Not maintaining skills: Competence
• Accepting inappropriate gift: Integrity

**Escalation Order:**
1. Internal policies
2. Immediate supervisor
3. Higher management
4. Board/Audit committee
5. IMA Ethics Counselor
6. Attorney
7. Resignation (last resort)

**Remember:**
Confidentiality continues AFTER employment ends
Can't share secrets even after resignation

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Four principles: Honesty, Fairness, Objectivity, Responsibility
Four standards: Competence, Confidentiality, Integrity, Credibility
Competence: Maintain professional skills
Confidentiality: Protect information, don't use for personal gain
Integrity: Avoid/disclose conflicts, refuse inappropriate gifts
Credibility: Communicate fairly, disclose ALL relevant info
Resolve conflicts by escalating through proper channels

knowledgeCheck.question: A CMA discovers their manager is overstating revenue. According to IMA guidelines, the CMA's first step should be to:
options: Follow established internal policies and discuss with the manager
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The IMA's recommended resolution process starts with following established organizational policies and discussing with the supervisor (unless they're involved in the issue). External reporting and resignation are later steps if internal resolution fails.
knowledgeCheck.hint: What's the first step in the IMA's conflict resolution process?

title: Sarbanes-Oxley and Internal Controls
description: Understand SOX requirements and compliance

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: SOX changed corporate governance forever! CMAs at public companies work within SOX requirements daily, documenting controls, testing effectiveness, and ensuring compliance. Understanding SOX is essential for financial reporting roles.

sections.title: Sarbanes-Oxley Background
sections.content:
**Why SOX Was Enacted (2002):**
• Response to Enron, WorldCom scandals
• Restore investor confidence
• Improve corporate governance
• Strengthen financial reporting

**Applies to:**
• U.S. public companies (SEC registrants)
• Foreign companies listed on U.S. exchanges
• Some provisions affect private companies

**Key Oversight:**
• PCAOB (Public Company Accounting Oversight Board)
• Oversees auditors of public companies
• Sets auditing standards

sections.title: Key SOX Sections
rows: Sign off on financial reports and controls
Evaluate and report on internal controls

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: SOX Section 404
sections.content:
**The Most Significant Section:**

**Management Responsibilities:**
• Design internal controls over financial reporting (ICFR)
• Document controls
• Test control effectiveness
• Identify deficiencies
• Include assessment in annual report

**Auditor Responsibilities:**
• Audit internal controls (integrated audit)
• Issue opinion on ICFR effectiveness
• Report material weaknesses

**Control Deficiency Levels:**
• **Deficiency:** Small issue
• **Significant Deficiency:** Important, not material
• **Material Weakness:** Could result in material misstatement

**Memory:** '404 = Four Oh Four = Control Error Page'

sections.title: COSO Internal Control Framework
sections.content:
**COSO: Framework for SOX Compliance**

**5 Components:**
• **Control Environment:** Tone at the top
• **Risk Assessment:** Identify and analyze risks
• **Control Activities:** Policies and procedures
• **Information & Communication:** Quality information flow
• **Monitoring:** Ongoing evaluation

**17 Principles:**
Each component has underlying principles

**Documentation Required:**
• Flowcharts of processes
• Control matrices
• Risk and control mapping
• Testing documentation
• Evidence of management review

sections.title: SOX Compliance Process
sections.content:
**Annual SOX Compliance Cycle:**

**1. Scoping:**
• Identify significant accounts
• Identify relevant assertions
• Determine key controls

**2. Documentation:**
• Update process narratives/flowcharts
• Confirm control descriptions
• Document risks and responses

**3. Testing:**
• Design tests of controls
• Execute testing (walkthroughs, samples)
• Document results

**4. Evaluation:**
• Identify deficiencies
• Assess severity
• Determine remediation

**5. Reporting:**
• Management report on ICFR
• Auditor attestation

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Control Deficiencies
sections.content:
**Severity Levels:**

**Material Weakness:**
• Reasonable possibility of material misstatement
• NOT prevented or detected timely
• MUST be disclosed in annual report
• Adverse opinion on ICFR

**Significant Deficiency:**
• Less severe than material weakness
• Still important enough to report
• Reported to audit committee
• Not necessarily public

**Control Deficiency:**
• Control doesn't operate as designed
• May not rise to significant level

**Key Point:**
One material weakness = ICFR is NOT effective
Even if all other controls are perfect

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SOX: Response to early 2000s accounting scandals
Section 302: CEO/CFO certifications
Section 404: Management assessment and auditor attestation
COSO framework: 5 components for internal control
Material weakness must be publicly disclosed
PCAOB oversees auditors of public companies
CMAs heavily involved in SOX compliance documentation and testing

knowledgeCheck.question: Under SOX Section 404, if a company's auditor identifies a material weakness in internal control over financial reporting, the auditor must:
options: Issue an unqualified opinion with emphasis of matter
Issue an adverse opinion on internal control
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A material weakness in ICFR means internal control is NOT effective, requiring an adverse opinion on internal controls. The auditor may still issue an unqualified opinion on the financial statements themselves if they are fairly stated despite the control weakness.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Material weakness = control is not effective = what type of opinion?

title: Data Privacy and GDPR Compliance
description: Understand privacy regulations and compliance requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters for Management Accountants
sections.content: Data privacy is a business imperative! With GDPR fines reaching billions of dollars, CMAs must understand privacy requirements. Finance handles sensitive personal data, making compliance a direct responsibility.

sections.title: Privacy Landscape
sections.content:
**Major Regulations:**
• **GDPR:** EU General Data Protection Regulation
• **CCPA/CPRA:** California Consumer Privacy Act
• **HIPAA:** US healthcare data
• **GLBA:** US financial data
• **PIPEDA:** Canada privacy law

**Why It Matters:**
• Heavy fines for non-compliance
• Customer trust and reputation
• Competitive advantage
• Legal liability

**Trend:**
Privacy regulations spreading globally
Companies often apply strictest standard everywhere

sections.title: GDPR Key Requirements
rows: Must have legal reason to process data

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: GDPR Principles
sections.content:
**Seven GDPR Principles:**

**1. Lawfulness, Fairness, Transparency:**
Process legally and openly

**2. Purpose Limitation:**
Collect for specific, stated purposes

**3. Data Minimization:**
Only what's needed, nothing more

**4. Accuracy:**
Keep data up to date

**5. Storage Limitation:**
Don't keep longer than necessary

**6. Integrity & Confidentiality:**
Secure the data

**7. Accountability:**
Be able to demonstrate compliance

**Memory:** 'LPDASIA'
Lawful, Purpose, Data-min, Accuracy, Storage, Integrity, Accountable

sections.title: GDPR Penalties
sections.content:
**Two Tiers of Fines:**

**Tier 1 (Lower):**
• Up to €10 million or 2% global revenue
• Technical/organizational violations
• Record-keeping, security failures

**Tier 2 (Higher):**
• Up to €20 million or 4% global revenue
• Fundamental violations
• Consent, rights violations

**Notable Fines:**
• Meta (Facebook): €1.2 billion (2023)
• Amazon: €746 million
• Google: €90 million

**Beyond Fines:**
• Reputational damage
• Customer loss
• Class action lawsuits

sections.title: Finance Department Implications
sections.content:
**Data Handled by Finance:**
• Employee personal data (payroll)
• Customer payment information
• Vendor contact details
• Tax identification numbers

**CMA Responsibilities:**
• Data inventory and mapping
• Retention policy compliance
• Vendor due diligence
• Privacy impact assessments
• Breach cost estimation

**Best Practices:**
• Minimize data collection
• Encrypt sensitive data
• Control access
• Document processing activities
• Train staff regularly

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: GDPR vs CCPA
sections.content:
**Key Differences:**

**GDPR (EU):**
• Applies to EU residents' data
• Opt-in consent required
• No revenue threshold
• Broader scope

**CCPA (California):**
• California residents only
• Opt-out model (can sell, must allow opt-out)
• Applies to businesses with $25M+ revenue
• Right to know what's sold

**Common Ground:**
• Right to access data
• Right to deletion
• Transparency requirements
• Penalties for violations

**Exam Tip:**
GDPR = Opt-IN (stricter)
CCPA = Opt-OUT

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: GDPR: EU privacy regulation with global impact
Fines up to €20M or 4% global revenue
Key rights: Access, erasure, portability, objection
Consent must be clear and affirmative
72-hour breach notification required
Finance handles significant personal data
GDPR = Opt-in; CCPA = Opt-out model

knowledgeCheck.question: Under GDPR, how quickly must an organization report a personal data breach to the supervisory authority?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: GDPR requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, unless the breach is unlikely to result in risk to individuals. Affected individuals must also be notified without undue delay if high risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: GDPR has a specific short timeframe for breach reporting.
